Portable idea: when a weighted tree can be realized as such a graph lattice by adding a root and extra root-edges, irreducibility of original vertex classes becomes a connectedness-of-complement question.
  • [arXiv:1010.6257 Proposition 3.4] Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected loopless undirected multigraph with no cut-edge. Fix a root r∈V and identify Γ(G) with the subgroup of Γ̄(G) spanned by [v] for v≠r. Let T⊂V−{r}. Suppose [T]=y+z in Γ(G) and ⟨y,z⟩=−1. Then one of the following holds. (1) The induced subgraph G|T contains a cut-edge e; if deleting e splits G|T into vertex sets T1 and T2, then {y,z}={[T1],[T2]}. (2) The induced subgraph G|(V−T) contains a cut-edge e; if deleting e splits G|(V−T) into vertex sets T1 and T2 with r∈T2, then {y,z}={[T1∪T],−[T1]}.  (from: literature_1010.6257)
    Proof sketch: Write z=[T]−y. Then ⟨y,z⟩=−1 is equivalent to ⟨y,y−[T]⟩=1. Use the same edge-sum identity as in Proposition 3.3: ⟨y,y−[T]⟩ is the sum of squares (y_u−y_v)^2 over edges internal to components of G|T and G|(V−T), plus terms (y_u−y_v)(y_u−y_v−1) over cut edges between T and V−T. Each cut term is 0 or at least 2, and each square is nonnegative. Therefore the total value 1 must arise from a unique internal edge e on which the y-values differ by exactly 1; all other terms vanish. Hence e is a bridge in one of the induced subgraphs G|T or G|(V−T), and y is constant on each component after e is removed. If e lies in G|T, let T1,T2 be the two vertex sets after deleting e. Because G itself has no cut-edge, each Ti has at least one edge to V−T. Vanishing of all cut-edge terms forces the y-values on T1 and T2 to be 0 and 1 in some order, while y is 0 on the component of V−T containing the root. Thus y=[T1] or [T2], and the decomposition is [T]=[T1]+[T2]. If e lies in G|(V−T), let T2 be the component containing r and T1 the other component. Again, since e is not a global cut-edge, both T1 and T2 connect to T; the vanishing conditions force either y=−[T1] or y=[T∪T1]. This gives the second listed form. The no-cut-edge hypothesis is used exactly to force the existence of edges from both sides of the local bridge to the opposite side of the cut T,V−T, which pins down the constants.
  • [arXiv:1010.6257 Corollary 3.5] Let L=⊕_k L_k be an orthogonal direct sum of linear lattices. Here each L_k has an ordered basis x_1,…,x_n with ⟨x_i,x_i⟩=a_i≥2, ⟨x_i,x_{i+1}⟩=−1, and all other off-diagonal pairings 0. In one summand, an interval means a nonempty consecutive set T={x_i,…,x_j}, and [T]=Σ_{x∈T}x. If two intervals T,T′ in the same summand overlap but do not have the same left endpoint or the same right endpoint, write T⋔T′. A vector v is breakable if v=y+z with |y|,|z|≥3 and ⟨y,z⟩=−1; otherwise it is unbreakable. Then: (1) the irreducible vectors in L are precisely the vectors ±[T], where T is an interval in one summand L_k; (2) each summand L_k is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum of nonzero lattices; (3) if T⋔T′, then [T−T′]+[T′−T] and [T−T′]−[T′−T] are reducible, interpreting [A] as the sum over the basis elements in A; (4) [T] is unbreakable if and only if T contains at most one basis vertex x_i with a_i≥3, equivalently at most one vertex of degree at least 3 in the associated graph model.  (from: literature_1010.6257)
    Proof sketch: Model a linear lattice as a graph lattice: take a root r, a chain of vertices x_1,…,x_n, edges x_i x_{i+1}, and enough parallel edges from r to x_i so that the degree of x_i is a_i. An orthogonal sum of linear lattices is obtained by wedging the corresponding chains at the same root. Applying Proposition 3.3, a connected cut T not containing the root whose complement is connected must lie entirely in one chain and must be a consecutive interval; this proves the classification of irreducibles. For indecomposability, the vertex basis elements x_i are irreducible and their pairing graph is connected within each chain. In any orthogonal decomposition, an irreducible vector lies wholly in one summand; connectivity forces all x_i in a chain into the same summand, so L_k is indecomposable. For (3), if overlapping intervals T,T′ do not share an endpoint, their symmetric difference is a union of two separated pieces in the chain (or one interval split into two separated pieces when one interval lies strictly inside the other). The corresponding two nonzero sums are orthogonal, so either sign choice gives a nontrivial decomposition with inner product 0, hence a reducible vector. For (4), if T contains two vertices of degree at least 3, split T at an edge between them into consecutive intervals T1,T2, each with norm at least 3; then [T]=[T1]+[T2] and ⟨[T1],[T2]⟩=−1, so [T] is breakable. Conversely, if [T]=y+z is a breaking with ⟨y,z⟩=−1, Proposition 3.4 applies to the associated graph, which has no cut-edge. The alternative in which the bridge lies in the complement of T gives one side of norm 2, so it cannot be a breaking with both norms at least 3. Thus the bridge lies inside T, splitting it into two intervals both of norm at least 3; each such interval must contain a degree ≥3 vertex. Hence T contains at least two high-degree vertices. This proves the equivalence.
  • [arXiv:1412.0906 Lemma 3.1] Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, allowing multiple edges. Let Λ(G) be the graph lattice: start with the free abelian group on V, impose the relation [V]=Σ_{v∈V}v=0, and use the bilinear form induced by u·u=d(u) and u·v=-e(u,v) for u≠v, where d(u) is the valence of u and e(u,v) is the number of edges between u and v. For R⊆V write [R] for the class of Σ_{v∈R}v in Λ(G). A nonzero vector z in a positive-definite lattice is called irreducible if there do not exist nonzero vectors x,y with z=x+y and x·y≥0. Then a nonzero vector z∈Λ(G) is irreducible if and only if z=[R] for some nonempty proper subset R⊂V such that both induced subgraphs G[R] and G[V\R] are connected.  (from: literature_1412.0906)
    Proof sketch: Use the Laplacian/energy model of the graph lattice: an element is an integer-valued function on V modulo constants, and the pairing is Σ_{edges ab}(f(a)-f(b))(g(a)-g(b)). For necessity, represent z by an integer function f with min f=0. If max f=m≥2, let R_i={v: f(v)≥i}. Then z=Σ_{i=1}^m [R_i]. Since R_j⊆R_i for i<j, one has [R_i]·[R_j]=e(R_j,V\R_i)≥0; hence z=[R_1]+Σ_{i≥2}[R_i] is a nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative pairing, contradicting irreducibility. Thus max f=1 and z=[R]. If G[R] is disconnected, split R=A⊔B with no edges between A and B; then [R]=[A]+[B] and [A]·[B]=0. If G[V\R] is disconnected, use [R]=-[V\R] and the same argument on the complement. Hence both sides must be connected. Conversely, suppose R and V\R are connected and [R]=a+b. Choose integer functions f,g with f+g=1_R modulo constants. For each edge, orient so δ(1_R) is 0 or 1. The contribution to a·b is δf·δg. If the edge stays on one side of the cut, δg=-δf and the contribution is -(δf)^2≤0. If the edge crosses the cut, δg=1-δf and the contribution is δf(1-δf)≤0 because δf is integral. Thus a·b≤0. If a·b≥0, every edge contribution is 0. Then f is constant on G[R] and on G[V\R], and across each cut edge the constant difference is 0 or 1. Since both sides are connected, f represents either 0 or [R] modulo constants, so one of a,b is zero. This proves irreducibility.
  • [arXiv:1412.0906 Proposition 3.4] Let L be a positive-definite integral lattice of rank r. An obtuse superbase for L is a set B={v_0,...,v_r}⊂L such that v_1,...,v_r form a Z-basis of L, v_0+...+v_r=0, and v_i·v_j≤0 for every i≠j. Given such B, define a finite undirected graph G_B with vertex set B and exactly |v_i·v_j|=-v_i·v_j edges between v_i and v_j for i≠j. Then G_B is connected, and L is isomorphic, as an integral lattice, to the graph lattice Λ(G_B).  (from: literature_1412.0906)
    Proof sketch: First prove connectedness. If R is the vertex set of a nonempty connected component of G_B, let s_R=Σ_{x∈R}x∈L. For any y∈B outside R there are no edges from y to R, so y·s_R=0. For y∈R, use Σ_{x∈B}x=0 and the absence of edges from R to B\R to get y·s_R=-y·Σ_{x∉R}x=0. Thus s_R pairs trivially with every element of B. Since B spans L and L is positive definite, s_R=0. But because v_1,...,v_r are a basis and v_0=-Σ_{i=1}^r v_i, the only linear relation among the r+1 elements of B is an integral multiple of Σ_{x∈B}x=0. Hence no proper nonempty subcollection can sum to zero, so R=B and G_B is connected. Now define a homomorphism from the free abelian group on the vertices of G_B to L by sending each graph vertex to the corresponding vector in B. The total vertex sum maps to 0, so it descends to Λ(G_B). It is surjective because v_1,...,v_r are a basis, and the ranks agree because G_B is connected. The pairing is preserved: for distinct vertices it is preserved by the definition of edge multiplicity, and for a vertex v_k the graph valence is Σ_{i≠k}(-v_k·v_i)=v_k·v_k, using v_0+...+v_r=0. Hence the descended map is an isomorphism of lattices.
  • [arXiv:1412.0906 Lemma 3.5] Let L be an indecomposable positive-definite integral lattice, meaning L is not an orthogonal direct sum of two nonzero sublattices, and suppose L has an obtuse superbase B. Let v∈B and suppose v=x+y for some x,y∈L with x·y=-1. Then there are unique vertices u_1,u_2∈B\{v} such that u_1·x>0 and u_2·y>0, respectively, and the set B'=(B\{v,u_1,u_2})∪{x,y,u_1+u_2} is also an obtuse superbase for L.  (from: literature_1412.0906)
    Proof sketch: Use Proposition 3.4 to identify L with the graph lattice of the graph G_B associated to the obtuse superbase B. The paper uses Lemma 3.2, which says that indecomposability of L is equivalent to G_B being 2-connected. Apply Lemma 3.3 to the 2-connected graph G_B, the vertex v, and the decomposition v=x+y with x·y=-1. That lemma gives a cut edge e in G_B\{v}; after removing e, the graph G_B\{v} splits into two connected sides R and S, with endpoints u_1∈R and u_2∈S. Up to interchanging x and y, one has x=v+[R] and y=v+[S] in the graph lattice. The endpoints u_1,u_2 are characterized by x·u_1=1 and y·u_2=1, and no other vertex distinct from v,u_1,u_2 pairs positively with x or y; this gives the required uniqueness. The new set B' has total sum zero because x+y=v, so replacing v,u_1,u_2 by x,y,u_1+u_2 preserves the superbase relation. The cut-edge description also gives the needed unimodular change of spanning set: R∪S=B\{v}, and the replacement is the standard graph-lattice move corresponding to splitting along the cut edge and replacing its endpoints by their sum. Finally check obtuseness. Existing pairings among vertices not removed stay nonpositive. Lemma 3.3 gives w·x≤0 and w·y≤0 for every old vertex w outside {v,u_1,u_2}. Also x·y=-1. Since B was obtuse, (u_1+u_2) pairs nonpositively with all remaining old vertices. The only delicate pairings are with x and y: the unique cut edge between u_1 and u_2 contributes a negative term that cancels the unique positive endpoint pairing, giving (u_1+u_2)·x≤0 and (u_1+u_2)·y≤0. Hence all distinct elements of B' have nonpositive pairing, and B' is an obtuse superbase.
  • [arXiv:0709.0841 §2.2.3 (Positive cones and sign of the dual basis)] Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing or resolution graph, and let L=⊕_{j∈J} Z E_j be its intersection lattice, with symmetric form ( , ). Thus the intersection matrix I=((E_i,E_j)) is negative definite, has negative diagonal entries, and has non-negative off-diagonal entries given by incidences of the graph. Put L_Q=L⊗Q. For each j, let D_j∈L_Q be the dual element characterized by (D_j,E_i)=δ_{ji} for every i. Define the effective cone L_Q,e={Σ_j r_j E_j : r_j≥0 for all j} and the numerically effective cone L_Q,ne={x∈L_Q : (x,E_j)≥0 for all j}. Then L_Q,ne is exactly the positive cone generated by the D_j: L_Q,ne={Σ_j r_j D_j : r_j≥0 for all j}. Moreover, because I is negative definite and Γ is connected, every coefficient of every D_j in the basis {E_i} is strictly negative. Consequently −L_Q,ne⊂L_Q,e; in fact, every nonzero element of L_Q,ne has strictly negative E_i-coefficients.  (from: literature_0709.0841)
    Proof sketch: The equality L_Q,ne={Σ r_j D_j} is just dual-basis linear algebra: if x=Σ r_j D_j then (x,E_i)=r_i, and conversely r_i=(x,E_i). The sign assertion is the key maximum-principle/M-matrix fact. Write A=−I. Then A is symmetric positive definite, has positive diagonal entries, non-positive off-diagonal entries, and is irreducible because Γ is connected. Hence A is a nonsingular irreducible M-matrix, so A^{-1} has strictly positive entries. Equivalently, solve Ax=e_j. The solution minimizes 1/2 z^T A z−e_j^T z; replacing z by its positive part cannot increase this functional when off-diagonal entries are non-positive, so x≥0. If some coordinate x_i were 0, the equation (Ax)_i=0 for i≠j forces all adjacent coordinates also 0; connectedness propagates this to x_j=0, contradicting (Ax)_j=1. Thus x>0. Since D_j=I^{-1}e_j=−A^{-1}e_j, all coefficients of D_j are strictly negative. The inclusion −L_Q,ne⊂L_Q,e follows by taking non-negative combinations. In the original positive-definite-tree setting, this is the same statement after negating the form on a branch: inverse matrices of positive definite Stieltjes branch matrices have positive entries.
  • [arXiv:0709.0841 §2.2.4 (Canonical liftings to the effective and nef cones)] Keep the notation of §2.2.3, and let L′ be the dual lattice embedded in L_Q. Let H=L′/L. Use the coefficientwise partial order on L_Q: x≤y means y−x∈L_Q,e. For each class h=l′+L∈H there is a unique minimal effective representative l′_e(h)∈(l′+L)∩L_Q,e. The set of all these representatives is Q={Σ_j r_j E_j∈L′ : 0≤r_j<1 for every j}. Similarly, for each h=l′+L∈H, the intersection (l′+L)∩L_Q,ne has a unique maximal element, denoted l′_ne(h), and the intersection (l′+L)∩(−L_Q,ne) has a unique minimal element, denoted overline{l′}_ne(h). These two nef liftings satisfy overline{l′}_ne(h)=−l′_ne(−h).  (from: literature_0709.0841)
    Proof sketch: For the effective representative, write any representative in E-coordinates and reduce each coordinate modulo Z into the interval [0,1). This gives an element of Q in the same L-coset. Any other effective representative has the same fractional parts and differs from it by an integral cycle with all coefficients ≥0, so the element in Q is the unique coefficientwise minimum. For the nef representative, non-emptiness follows from the existence of an effective integral Z with (Z,E_j)<0 for all j: for m≫0, l′−mZ has all pairings with E_j positive. Such a Z is obtained from §2.2.3 by taking a positive integral multiple of −Σ_j D_j. Every nef element is anti-effective by −L_Q,ne⊂L_Q,e, hence all elements of a fixed nef coset are coefficientwise bounded above. The crucial closure property is: if x,y are nef representatives in the same coset, then z=max(x,y) coefficientwise is also nef. Indeed write z=x+a=y+b with a,b effective and with disjoint supports. For any vertex E_j, at least one of a_j,b_j is 0. If a_j=0, then (a,E_j)≥0 because a is effective and has no E_j term, while off-diagonal intersections are non-negative; hence (z,E_j)=(x,E_j)+(a,E_j)≥0. The case b_j=0 is identical. Thus z is nef. Since the coset is discrete and bounded above, choose a nef representative maximizing the sum of its coefficients; closure under max then shows it dominates every other nef representative, giving the unique maximum l′_ne(h). Applying this to −h and negating gives the unique minimal element in the negative nef cone and the identity overline{l′}_ne(h)=−l′_ne(−h).
  • [arXiv:0709.0841 Proposition 6.1.4 (Minimal nef reduction and generalized Laufer algorithm)] Let π:(X_tilde,E)→(X,0) be a good resolution of a normal surface singularity whose exceptional intersection form is negative definite. Let L=⊕_{j∈J} Z E_j, let L′⊂L_Q be the dual lattice, let L_e be the semigroup of effective integral cycles, and let L_Q,ne={x∈L_Q : (x,E_j)≥0 for all j}. For any l′∈L′ there exists a unique minimal element l_{l′}∈L_e such that e(l′):=l′−l_{l′} lies in L_Q,ne. This cycle is obtained by the following algorithm. Start with x_0=0. If l′−x_i∈L_Q,ne, stop and set t=i. Otherwise choose any index j(i) with (l′−x_i,E_{j(i)})<0 and define x_{i+1}=x_i+E_{j(i)}. The algorithm stops after finitely many steps and its final value is x_t=l_{l′}, independently of the choices made. Moreover, if mathcal L is a holomorphic line bundle on X_tilde with c_1(mathcal L)=l′, and K is the canonical cycle, with χ(l)=−(l,l+K)/2 for l∈L, then h^1(mathcal L)=h^1(mathcal L⊗O_{X_tilde}(−l_{l′}))−(l′,l_{l′})−χ(l_{l′}). In particular, the computation of h^1 for an arbitrary Chern class reduces, up to the displayed explicit lattice term, to the case of a line bundle whose Chern class is nef.  (from: literature_0709.0841)
    Proof sketch: Existence: choose an effective integral Z with (Z,E_j)<0 for every j, possible by the inverse-sign result of §2.2.3. Then for m≫0, l′−mZ is nef, so at least one effective l with l′−l nef exists. If l_1,l_2 are two such effective cycles, let l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise and write x_i=l_i−l. The supports of x_1 and x_2 are disjoint. For a fixed E_j, choose i with coefficient of E_j in x_i equal to 0; then (x_i,E_j)≥0 because x_i is effective and has no E_j term. Hence (l′−l,E_j)=(l′−l_i,E_j)+(x_i,E_j)≥0. Thus the coefficientwise minimum of two admissible cycles is admissible, giving a unique minimal admissible cycle l_{l′}. Algorithm: prove inductively that every x_i constructed satisfies x_i≤l_{l′}. If x_i≤l_{l′} but x_i+E_j≤l_{l′} failed for a chosen j, then E_j has coefficient 0 in l_{l′}−x_i, so (l_{l′}−x_i,E_j)≥0. Since l′−l_{l′} is nef, this gives (l′−x_i,E_j)≥0, contradicting the rule for choosing j. Therefore the algorithm is bounded above by l_{l′}; since one coefficient increases by 1 at each step, it terminates. Its terminal x_t is admissible, hence by minimality equals l_{l′}. For the cohomological formula, along the algorithm use exact sequences 0→mathcal L⊗O(−x_{i+1})→mathcal L⊗O(−x_i)→mathcal L⊗O_{E_{j(i)}}(−x_i)→0. The chosen inequality says deg(mathcal L⊗O_{E_{j(i)}}(−x_i))=(l′−x_i,E_{j(i)})<0, so H^0 of the curve term vanishes. Therefore the change in h^1 is minus the Euler characteristic of that curve term, equal to −(l′,x_{i+1}−x_i)+χ(x_i)−χ(x_{i+1}). Summing over i telescopes to the stated formula.
  • [arXiv:math/0310083 Lemma 5.4 and Proposition 5.7] Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with vertex set J and lattice L=⊕_{j∈J} Z b_j, where (b_j,b_j)=e_j and (b_i,b_j)=1 if i and j are adjacent and 0 otherwise. Let K∈L' be the canonical characteristic element, K(b_j)=−e_j−2. For any characteristic orbit [k]=K+2(l'+L), the intersection (l'+L)∩S_Q has a unique minimal element, denoted l'_[k]. (from: literature_math/0310083)

Hence l'+l remains in S_Q. Closure under coefficientwise minimum gives a unique minimal element. For (a), if (l'_[k],b_j)≤b_j^2 for some j, then l'_[k]−b_j is still in S_Q: at b_j this is exactly the assumed inequality, at adjacent vertices the pairing drops by 1, and elsewhere it is unchanged. (from: literature_math/0310083)
    Proof sketch: Existence and uniqueness of y(x) are proved exactly as for the minimal anti-nef representative: the set of elements of S_[k] above x is nonempty by adding a large anti-nef effective cycle, and it is closed under coefficientwise minimum. For the algorithm, let y=y(x). The key induction is that every intermediate x_l satisfies x_l≤y. If x_l≤y and the algorithm chooses j with (x_l+l_min,b_j)>0, then the b_j-coefficient of x_l must be strictly smaller than that of y. Otherwise y−x_l is effective with zero b_j-coefficient, so (x_l+l_min,b_j)=(y+l_min,b_j)−(y−x_l,b_j)≤0, since y∈S_[k] and (y−x_l,b_j)≥0; contradiction. Hence adding b_j keeps x_{l+1}≤y.   • [arXiv:math/0310083 Lemmas 7.6–7.7 and Lemma 9.1] Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing tree with a distinguished vertex 0, and write J*=J∖{0}. Assume Γ is almost-rational with distinguished vertex 0, meaning that replacing e_0 by some e'_0≤e_0 gives a rational graph, i.e. a graph whose canonical function χ_K satisfies χ_K(z)≥1 for every nonzero effective integral cycle z. Fix a characteristic orbit [k], let l=l'_[k] be the minimal anti-nef representative, and set k=K+2l. For each integer i≥0, there is a unique minimal cycle x(i)∈L satisfying m_0(x(i))=i and (x(i)+l,b_j)≤0 for every j∈J*, where m_0 denotes the b_0-coefficient; moreover x(i)≥0. The rooted Laufer algorithm from x(i) to x(i+1) is: start with x_0=x(i), x_1=x(i)+b_0; for l≥1, if some j∈J* satisfies (x_l+l,b_j)>0, choose such a j=j(l) and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when all pairings with vertices in J* are ≤0. This terminates at x(i+1), and for l>0 one has χ_k(x_{l+1})≤χ_k(x_l), while χ_k(x(i)+b_0)−χ_k(x(i))=1−(x(i)+l,b_0); in particular the first step raises χ_k exactly when x(i)+l is anti-nef also at vertex 0. Under the almost-rational hypothesis, the following stronger conclusions hold for every i≥0: (a) if x∈L has m_0(x)=i, then χ_k(x)≥χ_k(x(i)); (b) if y>x(i) and m_0(y)=i, then for some vertex b_j in the support of y−x(i), with j∈J*, one has χ_k(y−b_j)≤χ_k(y); (c) in any rooted Laufer sequence from x(i) to x(i+1), every step after the initial b_0-step has exactly (x_l+l,b_{j(l)})=1 and hence χ_k(x_{l+1})=χ_k(x_l) for 0<l<t. Consequently χ_k(x(i)+b_0)=χ_k(x(i+1)).  (from: literature_math/0310083)
    Proof sketch: For existence of x(i), one first constructs some cycle with b_0-coefficient i and nonpositive pairings along J* by taking a large anti-nef cycle and subtracting a suitable multiple of b_0; uniqueness follows because the set of such cycles is closed under coefficientwise minimum. Effectivity uses minimality of l: if x(i)=x_1−x_2 with disjoint effective positive and negative parts, then l−x_2 remains in S_Q, forcing x_2=0. The rooted algorithm is the same bounded-above induction as Lemma 7.4, now only adding vertices in J*: x(i+1) is an upper bound, and a chosen positive pairing forces the chosen coefficient to be below that in x(i+1). The χ identities again come from χ_k(x+b_j)−χ_k(x)=1−(x+l,b_j). For the AR refinements, use that any cycle supported away from the distinguished vertex lies in a rational subgraph of the rational graph obtained by decreasing e_0; hence its canonical χ is positive when nonzero. For (a), write any x with m_0(x)=i as x(i)−y_1+y_2 with y_1,y_2≥0, disjoint support, and no b_0 in either support. Then χ_k(x)=χ_k(x(i)−y_1)+χ_K(y_2)+(y_1,y_2)−(x(i)+l,y_2)≥χ_k(x(i)−y_1), using rationality for χ_K(y_2), disjointness for (y_1,y_2)≥0, and anti-nefness of x(i)+l on J*. Lemma 7.8 then gives χ_k(x(i)−y_1)≥χ_k(x(i)). For (b), if every removal y−b_j raised χ_k, then for all b_j in supp(y−x(i)) one gets (y+l,b_j)≥b_j^2+2. Since (x(i)+l,b_j)≤0, this implies (y−x(i)+K,b_j)≥0 on the support; summing over coefficients gives χ_K(y−x(i))≤0, contradicting rationality of the relevant subgraph. For (c), set z_l=x_l−x(i). For l>0, the chosen inequality and anti-nefness of x(i)+l imply (z_l,b_{j(l)})>0. In the rational graph obtained by decreasing e_0, these same positive intersections occur, and the sequence can be completed to Laufer's computation of the fundamental cycle. Rationality forces all positive intersections in such a computation sequence to be exactly 1; hence (z_l,b_{j(l)})=1, and because (x_l+l,b_{j(l)}) is a positive integer differing from this by the nonpositive number (x(i)+l,b_{j(l)}), it equals 1. Thus χ_k is constant after the initial step. A graph with all non-distinguished vertices satisfying −e_j≥δ_j is AR by decreasing e_0 enough, which is why this lemma is directly portable to one-bad-vertex plumbing trees after sign reversal.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Naive real Schur/Legendre bound: Using only Phi_i(2c) <= c^2 alpha_i loses the alpha/4 parity slack when the Schur complement S is nonintegral.
  • Floor-only induction on even branch transforms: Bounds like Phi_i(2c) <= floor(c^2 alpha_i) are still not sharp enough in cases where c^2 alpha_i is integral but c A_i^{-1} e_{r_i} is not integral.
  • Leaf deletion: Irreducibility in a deleted subtree need not persist after reattaching a leaf, as shown by the two-vertex tree with weights 1 and 2.
  • Minimal parity-coset witness: It yields useful constraints such as connected support and local inequalities |y·v|<=w(v), but I did not extract an independent contradiction from them.
  • Multiple-bad generalization: Branches may have alpha>1 and nonzero equality vectors; an explicit multiple-bad example makes the chosen bad vertex reducible.
  • Prove the chosen bad vertex is irreducible without uniqueness of the bad vertex.: t2 gives an explicit positive-definite multiple-bad tree in which the selected bad vertex is reducible, so uniqueness is essential for that stronger claim.
  • Drop the uniqueness of the bad vertex: Then a component after deleting the chosen vertex may not satisfy the rooted nonbad hypotheses, and alpha_C can exceed 1.
  • Use only the global Schur-complement inequality for k=0 and k=1: The global upper bound is zero in these cases, so a separate branch equality analysis is needed to rule out nontrivial decompositions.
  • Use t2's multiple-bad example as a counterexample: It fails because only the selected bad vertex b is reducible; r and all leaves are irreducible.
  • Orient all reducible vertices toward efficient adjacent components: The orientation contradiction works for coefficient plus or minus 1 witnesses, but does not yet control high-odd witnesses with coefficient plus or minus 3, plus or minus 5, and so on.
  • Extend the dual parity lemma from nonbad rooted branches to arbitrary bad rooted branches: The dual parity inequality can fail for bad rooted branches with small Schur complement, even though corresponding primal integer violations are harder to realize.

## Verdict

Under the stipulated interpretation that there is **exactly one** bad vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\), the proof strategy is sound. I found no genuine counterexample or unrepaired gap. The cleanest final proof should use the t1 **dual-parity rooted branch lemma**, not the convex-envelope proof, and should explicitly isolate the \(k=0,1\) equality cases.

The recommended strengthened conclusion is:

> If \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible. Otherwise, the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

---

## 1. Basic norm and weight-one checks

For \(x=\sum n_v v\in L(T)\),

\[
x^2=\sum_v n_v^2 w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E}n_un_v\in \mathbb Z.
\]

Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\) has \(x^2>0\), hence \(x^2\in \mathbb Z_{>0}\).

If \(u\) has weight \(1\), then \(u^2=1\). Suppose

\[
u=a+c,\qquad a,c\neq 0,\qquad a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]

Then \(a^2,c^2\in \mathbb Z_{>0}\), so

\[
1=u^2=a^2+c^2+2a\cdot c\ge 1+1=2,
\]

a contradiction. Thus every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

So it remains to treat the case where all weights are at least \(2\).

---

## 2. Rooted branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite rooted weighted tree with root \(r\), positive definite Gram matrix \(A\), inverse \(G=A^{-1}\), and

\[
\alpha=G_{rr}.
\]

Assume the rooted nonbad hypotheses:

\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad \text{for }u\neq r.
\]

Then:

1. \(0<\alpha\le 1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in \mathbb Z^C\) satisfies
   \[
   \lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,\qquad
   \lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad (u\neq r),
   \]
   then
   \[
   \lambda^T G\lambda\ge \alpha,
   \]
   with equality iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. Consequently, for every \(x\in \mathbb Z^C\), writing \(c=x_r\) and \(Q=x^TAx\), and for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
   Equality holds iff
   \[
   x=kG e_r
   \quad\text{or}\quad
   x=(k-1)G e_r,
   \]
   with the displayed vector integral.

### Proof sketch of the dual-parity lemma

Induct on \(|C|\). Let the child branches of \(r\) be \(C_i\), with child roots \(r_i\), matrices \(A_i\), inverses \(G_i\), and

\[
\alpha_i=(G_i)_{r_i r_i}.
\]

Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: the child root loses its edge to \(r\), so it gains the required \(+1\) margin. By induction, \(\alpha_i\le 1\).

The Schur complement at \(r\) gives

\[
\alpha=\frac{1}{S},
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]

If \(r\) has \(m\) children, then

\[
S\ge w(r)-m\ge 1,
\]

so \(0<\alpha\le 1\).

Now write a parity vector as

\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]

where \(s\) is odd. Define

\[
a_i=e_{r_i}^T G_i\mu_i,\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^T G_i\mu_i,\qquad
A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]

The block inverse formula gives

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

Apply the induction hypothesis inside each branch to \(2\mu_i-e_{r_i}\) and \(2\mu_i+e_{r_i}\). Expanding both inequalities gives

\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]

Hence

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to check the scalar inequality

\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]

for \(S\ge 1\) and odd \(s\). Multiplying by \(S\), this is

\[
4S|A_0|+(s+2A_0)^2\ge 1.
\]

This is elementary: if \(s\) and \(2A_0\) have the same sign, then \(|s+2A_0|\ge |s|\ge 1\). If they have opposite signs, write \(a=|s|\ge 1\) and \(u=|2A_0|\). The left side becomes

\[
(a-u)^2+2Su\ge 1.
\]

Equality occurs only when \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm 1\). Then the previous inequalities force every \(a_i=0\) and every \(E_i=0\), hence every \(\mu_i=0\). Thus equality occurs iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).

The branch inequality follows by applying the dual-parity lemma to

\[
\lambda=2Ax-(2k-1)e_r.
\]

Then \(\lambda\) has the required parity pattern, and

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4Q-4(2k-1)c+(2k-1)^2\alpha.
\]

Thus

\[
4Q-4(2k-1)c+(2k-1)^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]

which rearranges to

\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality corresponds exactly to \(\lambda=\pm e_r\), giving the two stated alternatives.

---

## 3. Equality when \(k=0,1\)

Let

\[
y=G e_r.
\]

For \(k=1\), equality in the branch inequality means

\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=y.
\]

For \(k=0\), equality means

\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=-y.
\]

Therefore nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(y\in \mathbb Z^C\). But \(y_r=\alpha\), and \(0<\alpha\le 1\), so integrality forces \(\alpha=1\).

If \(\alpha=1\), then \(S=1\) in the Schur complement formula

\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]

Since

\[
S\ge w(r)-m\ge 1,
\]

equality forces \(w(r)=m+1\) and \(\alpha_i=1\) for every child branch. Descending recursively through the finite tree eventually reaches a one-vertex rooted branch with

\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(v)}=1,
\]

so \(w(v)=1\). Hence:

> If \(C\) contains no weight-one vertex, then equality in the branch inequality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(x=0\).

This is the key equality fact needed globally.

The even inequality from t1 is also correct, though not needed globally:

\[
2nc-Q\le n^2\alpha,
\]

with equality iff \(x=nG e_r\), obtained from

\[
0\le (x-nG e_r)^TA(x-nG e_r).
\]

---

## 4. Branches after deleting the unique bad vertex

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex with

\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]

Root \(T\) at \(b\), and let \(C\) range over the components of \(T-b\). Let \(r_C\) be the vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\), and let

\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_C r_C}.
\]

Because \(A_T\) is positive definite, every principal submatrix \(A_C\) is positive definite.

Because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\neq b\) satisfies

\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]

For the root \(r_C\),

\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]

so

\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]

For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),

\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]

so

\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]

Thus every branch \(C\) satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses.

Finally, the Schur complement of the full Gram matrix at \(b\) gives

\[
w(b)-\sum_C \alpha_C>0,
\]

so

\[
\sum_C \alpha_C<w(b).
\]

---

## 5. Global irreducibility of the bad vertex when there is no weight-one vertex

Assume now that \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. We prove \(b\) is irreducible.

Take any \(x\in L(T)\), and write

\[
x=k b+\sum_C x_C,
\]

where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and \(x_C\in L(C)\). Let \(c_C\) be the coefficient of \(r_C\) in \(x_C\), and let \(Q_C=x_C^2\).

A direct calculation gives

\[
x\cdot(b-x)
=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\left((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\right).
\]

Applying the branch inequality to each \(C\),

\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]

Therefore

\[
x\cdot(b-x)
\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C
=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\]

Since

\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0,
\]

if \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), so

\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

Thus no reducibility witness can have \(k\notin\{0,1\}\).

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the global upper bound is \(0\), and each individual branch term is also \(\le 0\). If

\[
x\cdot(b-x)\ge 0,
\]

then equality must hold in every branch. Since \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, each branch has no weight-one vertex, so the \(k=0,1\) equality analysis forces

\[
x_C=0
\]

for every branch \(C\).

Hence:

- if \(k=0\), then \(x=0\);
- if \(k=1\), then \(x=b\).

Therefore for every \(x\neq 0,b\),

\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

So there are no nonzero \(a,c\in L(T)\) with

\[
b=a+c,
\qquad
a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]

Thus \(b\) is irreducible.

Combining with the weight-one case proves the theorem.

---

## Recommended final formulation

The theorem should be stated as:

> Let \(T\) be a finite weighted tree whose associated integral form is positive definite. Suppose there is exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\). Then \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, if \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible; otherwise the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

The uniqueness hypothesis is essential for this proof because it ensures that every component of \(T-b\) satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses.

## Task t4 — Investigate the non-unique-bad interpretation
**Proven results:**
  • In the multiple-bad example from t2, the full form is positive definite, the vertex b is reducible, and every other vertex type r, ell_i, and p_j is irreducible. — Positive definiteness follows from Schur complements. The reducibility of b is witnessed by z=2r+ell_1+ell_2+ell_3. Irreducibility of r, ell_i, and p_j is checked using the parity-coset criterion together with exact Schur-complement inverse entries and small integer minimizations.
  • If every reducible vertex in a positive-definite weighted tree has a parity witness with coefficient plus or minus 1 at that vertex, then the tree cannot have all vertices reducible. — A coefficient plus or minus 1 witness orients the vertex toward an adjacent component with inverse-root entry at least 1. Following orientations in a finite tree gives a directed 2-cycle, but the two inverse-root entries across any edge have product strictly less than 1 by the Schur complement.
  • Any all-reducible counterexample, if one exists, must contain a vertex whose reducing parity witnesses all have coefficient of absolute value at least 3 at that vertex. — This is the contrapositive of the unit-coefficient orientation obstruction.
**Failed attempts:**
  • Use t2's multiple-bad example as a counterexample: It fails because only the selected bad vertex b is reducible; r and all leaves are irreducible.
  • Orient all reducible vertices toward efficient adjacent components: The orientation contradiction works for coefficient plus or minus 1 witnesses, but does not yet control high-odd witnesses with coefficient plus or minus 3, plus or minus 5, and so on.
  • Extend the dual parity lemma from nonbad rooted branches to arbitrary bad rooted branches: The dual parity inequality can fail for bad rooted branches with small Schur complement, even though corresponding primal integer violations are harder to realize.
**Progress notes:** No all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree was found. The t2 example shows that a chosen bad vertex can be reducible, but it still contains irreducible vertices. A genuine counterexample would need high-odd parity witnesses; ruling those out appears to be the remaining obstacle.

## Bottom line

I do **not** get an all-reducible counterexample from the multiple-bad example in t2. In fact, in that example the chosen bad vertex \(b\) is reducible, but the other bad vertex \(r\), the three weight-\(2\) leaves, and the four weight-\(3\) leaves are all irreducible.

More generally, I found a useful obstruction: if every reducible vertex has a parity-coset witness whose coefficient at that vertex is \(\pm1\), then an all-reducible positive-definite tree is impossible. Thus any genuine counterexample must involve at least one vertex whose only reducing parity witnesses have coefficient \(\pm3,\pm5,\dots\) at that vertex.

---

# 1. The t2 multiple-bad example

Recall the tree:

- \(b\) has weight \(4\), adjacent to \(r,p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4\);
- \(r\) has weight \(2\), adjacent to \(b,\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3\);
- each \(\ell_i\) has weight \(2\);
- each \(p_j\) has weight \(3\).

Thus
\[
d(b)=5,\quad w(b)=4,\qquad d(r)=4,\quad w(r)=2,
\]
so both \(b\) and \(r\) are bad.

## Positive definiteness

The branch at \(b\) through \(r\) is the \(D_4\)-type tree with central vertex \(r\) of weight \(2\) and three weight-\(2\) leaves. Its root inverse entry is
\[
\alpha_r=2.
\]
Each \(p_j\)-branch has inverse root entry \(1/3\). Hence the Schur complement at \(b\) is
\[
4-\left(2+4\cdot \frac13\right)=\frac23>0.
\]
So the whole form is positive definite.

---

# 2. Reducibility of \(b\)

Let
\[
z=2r+\ell_1+\ell_2+\ell_3.
\]
Then
\[
z^2=2,
\qquad
b\cdot z=-2.
\]
Therefore
\[
(b+z)\cdot(-z)=-b\cdot z-z^2=2-2=0.
\]
Thus
\[
b=(b+z)+(-z)
\]
is a reducibility witness. Equivalently,
\[
y=b+2z\in b+2L(T),\qquad y^2=b^2=4,\qquad y\neq \pm b.
\]

So \(b\) is reducible.

---

# 3. The four \(p_j\) are irreducible

By symmetry it is enough to treat \(p=p_1\).

Let \(C=T-p\), rooted at \(b\). One computes
\[
(A_C^{-1})_{bb}=1.
\]
Indeed, after deleting \(p_1\), the branches at \(b\) have inverse root entries
\[
2,\quad \frac13,\quad \frac13,\quad \frac13,
\]
so the Schur complement is
\[
4-\left(2+3\cdot \frac13\right)=1.
\]

Take any
\[
y\in p+2L(T).
\]
Write
\[
y=s p+2X,
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(X\in L(C)\). Let \(B\) be the coefficient of \(b\) in \(X\). Then
\[
y^2=3s^2+4X^2-4sB.
\]
Since \((A_C^{-1})_{bb}=1\), Cauchy gives
\[
X^2\ge B^2.
\]

If \(|s|\ge 3\), then
\[
y^2\ge 3s^2+4B^2-4|s||B|\ge 2s^2>3,
\]
so no reducing vector has \(|s|\ge3\).

Thus \(s=\pm1\). If \(s=1\), then \(y^2\le 3\) would imply
\[
X^2\le B.
\]
Using \(X^2\ge B^2\), this forces \(B=0\) or \(B=1\). If \(B=0\), then \(X=0\), giving \(y=p\). If \(B=1\), then one would need \(X^2=1\), hence equality in Cauchy. The equality vector is
\[
A_C^{-1}e_b
=
b+2r+\ell_1+\ell_2+\ell_3+\frac13(p_2+p_3+p_4),
\]
which is not integral. So \(B=1\) is impossible.

The case \(s=-1\) is identical, giving only \(y=-p\). Therefore each \(p_j\) is irreducible.

---

# 4. The three \(\ell_i\) are irreducible

By symmetry take \(\ell=\ell_1\). Let \(C=T-\ell\), rooted at \(r\). Its root inverse entry is
\[
\alpha=(A_C^{-1})_{rr}=\frac85.
\]
Indeed, the branches at \(r\) inside \(C\) are:

- two weight-\(2\) leaves, each with inverse entry \(1/2\);
- the \(b\)-side, namely \(b\) with four weight-\(3\) leaves, whose root inverse entry is
  \[
  \frac{1}{4-4/3}=\frac38.
  \]

Thus
\[
\alpha=\frac{1}{2-\frac12-\frac12-\frac38}=\frac85.
\]

Now take
\[
y=s\ell+2X,\qquad s\ \text{odd},\quad X\in L(C),
\]
and let \(R\) be the coefficient of \(r\) in \(X\). Then
\[
y^2=2s^2+4X^2-4sR.
\]
The real minimization bound gives
\[
y^2\ge s^2\left(2-\frac85\right)=\frac25s^2.
\]
Hence if \(|s|\ge3\), then
\[
y^2\ge \frac{18}{5}>2,
\]
so no reducing vector has \(|s|\ge3\).

Thus \(s=\pm1\). Suppose \(s=1\). Then \(y^2\le2\) implies
\[
X^2\le R.
\]
By Cauchy,
\[
X^2\ge \frac{R^2}{8/5}=\frac58R^2.
\]
So \(R\) can only be \(0\) or \(1\). If \(R=0\), then \(X=0\), giving \(y=\ell\).

If \(R=1\), write \(X\) as
\[
X=r+a\ell_2+b\ell_3+X_B,
\]
where \(X_B\) lies in the \(b\)-side and \(B\) is the coefficient of \(b\) in \(X_B\). Then
\[
X^2
=
2+(2a^2-2a)+(2b^2-2b)+(X_B^2-2B).
\]
For the \(b\)-side one has \(X_B^2-2B\ge0\): if \(B=1\), the minimum is \(4-2=2\); if \(B\ge2\), Cauchy gives \(X_B^2\ge \frac83B^2\ge2B\); and if \(B\le0\), the claim is immediate. Therefore
\[
X^2\ge2>1,
\]
contradicting \(X^2\le R=1\).

The case \(s=-1\) follows by replacing \(X\) with \(-X\). Hence each \(\ell_i\) is irreducible.

---

# 5. The bad vertex \(r\) is irreducible

Now remove \(r\). The components are:

- three weight-\(2\) leaves \(\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3\), each with inverse entry \(1/2\);
- the \(b\)-side, namely \(b\) with four weight-\(3\) leaves, with inverse entry \(3/8\).

Thus the total inverse-root sum seen from \(r\) is
\[
\Gamma=3\cdot\frac12+\frac38=\frac{15}{8}.
\]

Take
\[
y=s r+2X,\qquad s\ \text{odd},
\]
and let \(P\) be the sum of the coefficients of \(X\) at the four neighbors of \(r\). Then
\[
y^2=2s^2+4X^2-4sP.
\]
The real bound gives
\[
y^2\ge s^2(2-\Gamma)=\frac18s^2.
\]
So for \(|s|\ge5\),
\[
y^2\ge \frac{25}{8}>2.
\]

It remains to check \(s=\pm1,\pm3\).

For \(s=1\), \(y^2\le2\) implies \(X^2\le P\). But each weight-\(2\) leaf contribution satisfies
\[
2a^2-a\ge0,
\]
and the \(b\)-side satisfies
\[
X_B^2-B\ge0.
\]
Hence \(X^2-P\ge0\), with equality only when \(X=0\). Thus \(y=r\). Similarly \(s=-1\) gives only \(y=-r\).

For \(s=3\), we need to bound \(3P-X^2\). Each weight-\(2\) leaf contributes at most
\[
\max_{a\in\mathbb Z}(3a-2a^2)=1.
\]
The \(b\)-side contributes at most \(0\), because \(3B-X_B^2\le0\). Therefore
\[
3P-X^2\le3.
\]
Thus
\[
y^2=18-4(3P-X^2)\ge18-12=6>2.
\]
The case \(s=-3\) is the same. Hence \(r\) is irreducible.

So in the t2 example, **exactly \(b\) is reducible; every other vertex is irreducible.**

---

# 6. Structural obstruction to an all-reducible counterexample

Suppose \(u\) is reducible and has a parity-coset witness
\[
y=\varepsilon u+2z,\qquad \varepsilon\in\{\pm1\},
\]
with \(z\in L(T-u)\), \(z\neq0\), and
\[
y^2\le u^2.
\]
Write \(z=\sum_C z_C\) over the components \(C\) of \(T-u\), and let \(c_C\) be the coefficient of the root of \(C\). Then
\[
y^2-u^2
=
4\sum_C(z_C^2-\varepsilon c_C).
\]
Hence some component satisfies
\[
z_C^2\le \varepsilon c_C.
\]
In particular \(\varepsilon c_C>0\), and Cauchy gives
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C z_C^2\le \alpha_C \varepsilon c_C,
\]
so
\[
\alpha_C\ge1.
\]

Now take an edge \(uv\). Let \(\alpha_{u|v}\) and \(\alpha_{v|u}\) be the inverse-root entries of the two components obtained by deleting \(uv\), rooted at \(u\) and \(v\). Since the whole tree is positive definite, the Schur complement across the edge gives
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\alpha_{v|u}<1.
\]

Therefore, if every vertex had a reducing witness with coefficient \(\pm1\) at that vertex, we could orient each vertex toward a component with \(\alpha\ge1\). Any orientation of a finite tree with outdegree at least \(1\) at every vertex contains a directed \(2\)-cycle \(u\leftrightarrow v\). But that would force
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\ge1,\qquad \alpha_{v|u}\ge1,
\]
contradicting
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\alpha_{v|u}<1.
\]

So any all-reducible counterexample must contain at least one vertex whose reducing parity witnesses all have coefficient \(\pm3,\pm5,\dots\) at that vertex. This is a strong restriction.

---

# 7. Exact computational search framework

A rigorous search can be done using the parity criterion. For each vertex \(u\), enumerate all
\[
y\in u+2L(T)
\]
with
\[
y^2\le u^2.
\]
Since the form is positive definite, this is finite. A coordinate bound follows from Cauchy:
\[
|y_i|^2\le y^2\,(A^{-1})_{ii}\le w(u)(A^{-1})_{ii}.
\]

Here is exact Python/SymPy code implementing that search.

```python
import itertools
import math
import networkx as nx
import sympy as sp


def gram_matrix(G, weights):
    n = len(weights)
    A = sp.zeros(n)
    for i in range(n):
        A[i, i] = weights[i]
    for i, j in G.edges():
        A[i, j] = A[j, i] = -1
    return A


def is_positive_definite_exact(A):
    n = A.rows
    for k in range(1, n + 1):
        if A[:k, :k].det() <= 0:
            return False
    return True


def ceil_sqrt_rat(q):
    q = sp.Rational(q)
    num, den = int(q.p), int(q.q)
    r = math.isqrt((num + den - 1) // den)
    while r * r < q:
        r += 1
    return r


def q_value(A, y):
    v = sp.Matrix(y)
    return (v.T * A * v)[0]


def reducibility_witness(A, u):
    """
    Return a parity-coset witness y for reducibility of basis vector u,
    or None if no witness exists.
    """
    n = A.rows
    w_u = A[u, u]
    G = A.inv()

    bounds = []
    for i in range(n):
        bounds.append(ceil_sqrt_rat(w_u * G[i, i]))

    ranges = []
    for i, B in enumerate(bounds):
        vals = []
        for a in range(-B, B + 1):
            if i == u:
                if a % 2 != 0:
                    vals.append(a)
            else:
                if a % 2 == 0:
                    vals.append(a)
        ranges.append(vals)

    e_u = [0] * n
    e_u[u] = 1
    minus_e_u = [0] * n
    minus_e_u[u] = -1

    for y in itertools.product(*ranges):
        y = list(y)
        if y == e_u or y == minus_e_u:
            continue
        if q_value(A, y) <= w_u:
            return y
    return None


def analyze_tree(G, weights):
    A = gram_matrix(G, weights)
    if not is_positive_definite_exact(A):
        return None

    witnesses = {}
    irreducible = []
    reducible = []

    for u in range(len(weights)):
        wit = reducibility_witness(A, u)
        if wit is None:
            irreducible.append(u)
        else:
            reducible.append(u)
            witnesses[u] = wit

    return {
        "A": A,
        "irreducible": irreducible,
        "reducible": reducible,
        "witnesses": witnesses,
    }


def search(max_vertices=8, max_weight=6):
    for n in range(1, max_vertices + 1):
        for G in nx.generators.nonisomorphic_trees(n):
            deg = dict(G.degree())
            for weights in itertools.product(range(2, max_weight + 1), repeat=n):
                # Need at least one bad vertex.
                if not any(weights[v] < deg[v] for v in range(n)):
                    continue

                result = analyze_tree(G, weights)
                if result is None:
                    continue

                if len(result["irreducible"]) == 0:
                    print("ALL-REDUCIBLE COUNTEREXAMPLE FOUND")
                    print("n =", n)
                    print("edges =", sorted(G.edges()))
                    print("weights =", weights)
                    print("witnesses =", result["witnesses"])
                    return G, weights, result

    print("No all-reducible counterexample found in this range.")
    return None
```

This search is exact: positive definiteness, norms, and parity-coset witnesses are all checked over the rationals/integers.

---

# Current status

I found no concrete all-reducible counterexample. The multiple-bad example from t2 is not one: it still has irreducible vertices. The main structural obstruction is that a counterexample cannot be built only from the usual coefficient-\(\pm1\) reductions; it must use a genuinely higher odd parity witness somewhere.

My present conjecture is that the weaker “at least one bad vertex” interpretation is still true: every positive-definite weighted tree with at least one bad vertex has some irreducible vertex basis element. But the proof needs a way to rule out or control the high-odd parity witnesses.

\[

    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.

Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

Under the stipulated interpretation that there is **exactly one** bad vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\), the proof strategy is sound. I found no genuine counterexample or unrepaired gap. The cleanest final proof should use the t1 **dual-parity rooted branch lemma**, not the convex-envelope proof, and should explicitly isolate the \(k=0,1\) equality cases.

The recommended strengthened conclusion is:

> If \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible. Otherwise, the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

---

## 1. Basic norm and weight-one checks

For \(x=\sum n_v v\in L(T)\),

\[
x^2=\sum_v n_v^2 w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E}n_un_v\in \mathbb Z.
\]

Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\) has \(x^2>0\), hence \(x^2\in \mathbb Z_{>0}\).

If \(u\) has weight \(1\), then \(u^2=1\). Suppose

\[
u=a+c,\qquad a,c\neq 0,\qquad a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]

Then \(a^2,c^2\in \mathbb Z_{>0}\), so

\[
1=u^2=a^2+c^2+2a\cdot c\ge 1+1=2,
\]

a contradiction. Thus every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

So it remains to treat the case where all weights are at least \(2\).

---

## 2. Rooted branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite rooted weighted tree with root \(r\), positive definite Gram matrix \(A\), inverse \(G=A^{-1}\), and

\[
\alpha=G_{rr}.
\]

Assume the rooted nonbad hypotheses:

\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad \text{for }u\neq r.
\]

Then:

1. \(0<\alpha\le 1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in \mathbb Z^C\) satisfies
   \[
   \lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,\qquad
   \lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad (u\neq r),
   \]
   then
   \[
   \lambda^T G\lambda\ge \alpha,
   \]
   with equality iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. Consequently, for every \(x\in \mathbb Z^C\), writing \(c=x_r\) and \(Q=x^TAx\), and for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
   Equality holds iff
   \[
   x=kG e_r
   \quad\text{or}\quad
   x=(k-1)G e_r,
   \]
   with the displayed vector integral.

### Proof sketch of the dual-parity lemma

Induct on \(|C|\). Let the child branches of \(r\) be \(C_i\), with child roots \(r_i\), matrices \(A_i\), inverses \(G_i\), and

\[
\alpha_i=(G_i)_{r_i r_i}.
\]

Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: the child root loses its edge to \(r\), so it gains the required \(+1\) margin. By induction, \(\alpha_i\le 1\).

The Schur complement at \(r\) gives

\[
\alpha=\frac{1}{S},
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]

If \(r\) has \(m\) children, then

\[
S\ge w(r)-m\ge 1,
\]

so \(0<\alpha\le 1\).

Now write a parity vector as

\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]

where \(s\) is odd. Define

\[
a_i=e_{r_i}^T G_i\mu_i,\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^T G_i\mu_i,\qquad
A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]

The block inverse formula gives

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

Apply the induction hypothesis inside each branch to \(2\mu_i-e_{r_i}\) and \(2\mu_i+e_{r_i}\). Expanding both inequalities gives

\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]

Hence

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to check the scalar inequality

\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]

for \(S\ge 1\) and odd \(s\). Multiplying by \(S\), this is

\[
4S|A_0|+(s+2A_0)^2\ge 1.
\]

This is elementary: if \(s\) and \(2A_0\) have the same sign, then \(|s+2A_0|\ge |s|\ge 1\). If they have opposite signs, write \(a=|s|\ge 1\) and \(u=|2A_0|\). The left side becomes

\[
(a-u)^2+2Su\ge 1.
\]

Equality occurs only when \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm 1\). Then the previous inequalities force every \(a_i=0\) and every \(E_i=0\), hence every \(\mu_i=0\). Thus equality occurs iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).

The branch inequality follows by applying the dual-parity lemma to

\[
\lambda=2Ax-(2k-1)e_r.
\]

Then \(\lambda\) has the required parity pattern, and

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4Q-4(2k-1)c+(2k-1)^2\alpha.
\]

Thus

\[
4Q-4(2k-1)c+(2k-1)^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]

which rearranges to

\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality corresponds exactly to \(\lambda=\pm e_r\), giving the two stated alternatives.

---

## 3. Equality when \(k=0,1\)

Let

\[
y=G e_r.
\]

For \(k=1\), equality in the branch inequality means

\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=y.
\]

For \(k=0\), equality means

\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=-y.
\]

Therefore nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(y\in \mathbb Z^C\). But \(y_r=\alpha\), and \(0<\alpha\le 1\), so integrality forces \(\alpha=1\).

If \(\alpha=1\), then \(S=1\) in the Schur complement formula

\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]

Since

\[
S\ge w(r)-m\ge 1,
\]

equality forces \(w(r)=m+1\) and \(\alpha_i=1\) for every child branch. Descending recursively through the finite tree eventually reaches a one-vertex rooted branch with

\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(v)}=1,
\]

so \(w(v)=1\). Hence:

> If \(C\) contains no weight-one vertex, then equality in the branch inequality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(x=0\).

This is the key equality fact needed globally.

The even inequality from t1 is also correct, though not needed globally:

\[
2nc-Q\le n^2\alpha,
\]

with equality iff \(x=nG e_r\), obtained from

\[
0\le (x-nG e_r)^TA(x-nG e_r).
\]

---

## 4. Branches after deleting the unique bad vertex

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex with

\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]

Root \(T\) at \(b\), and let \(C\) range over the components of \(T-b\). Let \(r_C\) be the vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\), and let

\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_C r_C}.
\]

Because \(A_T\) is positive definite, every principal submatrix \(A_C\) is positive definite.

Because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\neq b\) satisfies

\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]

For the root \(r_C\),

\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]

so

\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]

For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),

\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]

so

\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]

Thus every branch \(C\) satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses.

Finally, the Schur complement of the full Gram matrix at \(b\) gives

\[
w(b)-\sum_C \alpha_C>0,
\]

so

\[
\sum_C \alpha_C<w(b).
\]

---

## 5. Global irreducibility of the bad vertex when there is no weight-one vertex

Assume now that \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. We prove \(b\) is irreducible.

Take any \(x\in L(T)\), and write

\[
x=k b+\sum_C x_C,
\]

where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and \(x_C\in L(C)\). Let \(c_C\) be the coefficient of \(r_C\) in \(x_C\), and let \(Q_C=x_C^2\).

A direct calculation gives

\[
x\cdot(b-x)
=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\left((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\right).
\]

Applying the branch inequality to each \(C\),

\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]

Therefore

\[
x\cdot(b-x)
\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C
=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\]

Since

\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0,
\]

if \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), so

\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

Thus no reducibility witness can have \(k\notin\{0,1\}\).

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the global upper bound is \(0\), and each individual branch term is also \(\le 0\). If

\[
x\cdot(b-x)\ge 0,
\]

then equality must hold in every branch. Since \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, each branch has no weight-one vertex, so the \(k=0,1\) equality analysis forces

\[
x_C=0
\]

for every branch \(C\).

Hence:

- if \(k=0\), then \(x=0\);
- if \(k=1\), then \(x=b\).

Therefore for every \(x\neq 0,b\),

\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

So there are no nonzero \(a,c\in L(T)\) with

\[
b=a+c,
\qquad
a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]

Thus \(b\) is irreducible.

Combining with the weight-one case proves the theorem.

---

## Recommended final formulation

The theorem should be stated as:

> Let \(T\) be a finite weighted tree whose associated integral form is positive definite. Suppose there is exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\). Then \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, if \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible; otherwise the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

The uniqueness hypothesis is essential for this proof because it ensures that every component of \(T-b\) satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses.

[verified ✓ | is_relaxation: False | problem_solved (verifier-canonical, self-contained): The proof establishes the claimed result under the stated/explicit interpretation that there is exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\): for any finite weighted tree \(T\) whose associated integral symmetric form is positive definite and has a unique such “bad” vertex, \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex of \(L(T)\). More specifically, if \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible; otherwise the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.]
Source: final_solutions.jsonl — verified+refined text, the artifact that actually survived the verify+refine loop.
## Original Problem

Let $T$ denote a finite, weighted tree with vertex set $V$, edge set $E$, and weight function $w : V \longrightarrow \bZ$.
Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following self-contained statement. Let \(T\) be a finite tree with vertex set \(V\), edge set \(E\), integer weight function \(w:V\to\mathbb Z\), and vertex degree \(d_T(v)\). Let \(L(T)\) be the free abelian group on \(V\), equipped with the symmetric bilinear form determined on vertices by
\[
u\cdot v=
\begin{cases}
w(u),&u=v,\\
-1,&u\ne v\text{ and }(u,v)\in E,\\
0,&u\ne v\text{ and }(u,v)\notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Assume this form is positive definite, and assume that there is exactly one vertex \(b\in V\) such that \(w(b)<d_T(b)\). An element \(y\in L(T)\) is called irreducible if there do not exist nonzero \(p,q\in L(T)\) with \(y=p+q\) and \(p\cdot q\ge 0\). Then \(T\) contains a vertex \(u\in V\) which is irreducible in \(L(T)\). This is the original problem with “a single vertex” interpreted in its literal uniqueness sense; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

For any \(z\in L(T)\), write \(z^2=z\cdot z\). Since the form is integral and positive definite, every nonzero \(z\in L(T)\) has
\[
z^2\in \mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

### Lemma 1. A norm-one vertex is irreducible

If \(u\in V\) satisfies \(w(u)=u^2=1\), then \(u\) is irreducible.

**Proof.** Suppose \(u=p+q\), where \(p,q\in L(T)\) are nonzero and \(p\cdot q\ge 0\). Then \(p^2,q^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), so
\[
1=u^2=(p+q)^2=p^2+q^2+2p\cdot q\ge 1+1=2,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore no such decomposition exists. \(\square\)

### A Schur-complement fact

We will use the following elementary matrix fact. Let
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & c^T\\
c & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Set
\[
S=a-c^TB^{-1}c.
\]
Then
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & c^TB^{-1}\\
0 & I
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
S & 0\\
0 & B
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0\\
B^{-1}c & I
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Hence \(M\) is positive definite iff \(S>0\). When \(S>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
S^{-1} & -S^{-1}c^TB^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}cS^{-1} & B^{-1}+B^{-1}cS^{-1}c^TB^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

### Lemma 2. Rooted nonbad branch inequality

Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at a vertex \(r\). Let \(d_C\) denote degree inside \(C\). Let \(A\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), and assume \(A\) is positive definite. Suppose
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad\text{for every }u\ne r.
\]
Let \(G=A^{-1}\), let \(e_r\) be the coordinate vector of the root, and set
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=e_r^TGe_r.
\]
Then for every integral vector \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), if \(c=x_r\) is its root coefficient and \(Q=x^TAx\), then for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]
Moreover, if \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), equality with \(x\ne 0\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex.

**Proof.** We first prove the dual parity estimate.

#### Dual parity estimate

We claim that
\[
0<\alpha\le 1,
\]
and that if \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) satisfies
\[
\lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,
\qquad
\lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad(u\ne r),
\]
then
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha,
\]
with equality iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).

We prove this by induction on \(|C|\). If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\), with \(w(r)\ge 1\). Thus \(\alpha=1/w(r)\le 1\). For odd \(\lambda_r=s\),
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=\frac{s^2}{w(r)}\ge \frac{1}{w(r)}=\alpha,
\]
with equality iff \(s=\pm1\), i.e. iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).

Now assume \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the components of \(C-r\), rooted at the vertices \(r_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\), \(G_i=A_i^{-1}\), \(e_i=e_{r_i}\), and
\[
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each rooted \(C_i\) satisfies the same hypotheses: its root \(r_i\) has lost the edge to \(r\), so
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and every other vertex has the same degree in \(C_i\) as in \(C\). Thus, by induction, \(\alpha_i\le 1\).

With the root \(r\) ordered first, the matrix \(A\) has block form
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where \(\eta\) has \(C_i\)-component \(e_i\). The Schur complement is
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
  =w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Since \(A\) is positive definite, \(S>0\), and
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac1S.
\]
Because \(m=d_C(r)\), the hypotheses give
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (m+1)-m=1.
\]
Hence \(\alpha\le 1\).

Now let \(\lambda\) have the required parity pattern. Write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s=\lambda_r\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
a_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula above,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

For each \(i\), the two vectors \(2\mu_i-e_i\) and \(2\mu_i+e_i\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates in \(C_i\). By induction,
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge \alpha_i,
\]
and
\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Expanding gives
\[
4E_i-4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]
Thus
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains only to prove the scalar inequality
\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]
for \(S\ge1\) and odd \(s\). Equivalently, setting \(y=2A_0\), we need
\[
(s+y)^2+2S|y|\ge 1.
\]
If \(sy\ge0\), then \(|s+y|=|s|+|y|\ge1\), so the inequality holds. If \(sy<0\), set \(a=|s|\ge1\) and \(u=|y|>0\). Then the left side is
\[
(a-u)^2+2Su.
\]
If \(a=1\), this equals
\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u>1.
\]
If \(a\ge3\), then either \(u\le a-1\), in which case \((a-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>a-1\), in which case \(2Su>2(a-1)\ge4\). Thus the inequality always holds.

Equality in the scalar inequality occurs only when \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). If equality holds in the dual parity estimate, then
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=\frac1S=\alpha.
\]
The equality conditions above force \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). Then
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=4\sum_i E_i+\frac1S,
\]
so equality also forces every \(E_i=0\). Since each \(G_i\) is positive definite, \(\mu_i=0\) for every \(i\). Hence \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). Conversely, \(\lambda=\pm e_r\) clearly gives equality. This proves the dual parity estimate.

#### Derivation of the branch inequality

Let \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), let \(c=x_r\), and let \(Q=x^TAx\). Fix \(k\in\mathbb Z\), and set
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-t e_r.
\]
Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Its root coordinate is odd, and every nonroot coordinate is even. Therefore the dual parity estimate gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Thus
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]
so
\[
tc-Q\le \frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1),
\]
and therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality occurs precisely when \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=ke_r,
\qquad
x=kA^{-1}e_r.
\]
If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then
\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,
\qquad
x=(k-1)A^{-1}e_r.
\]

Finally, suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), and equality holds with \(x\ne0\). Let
\[
y=A^{-1}e_r.
\]
For \(k=1\), the equality alternatives are \(x=y\) or \(x=0\); since \(x\ne0\), \(y\) is integral. For \(k=0\), the alternatives are \(x=0\) or \(x=-y\); again \(x\ne0\) implies \(y\) is integral. Hence
\[
\alpha=y_r
\]
is a positive integer. Since \(0<\alpha\le1\), we get \(\alpha=1\).

We now show that \(\alpha=1\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex. Use the Schur-complement notation from above:
\[
\alpha=\frac1S,
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
If \(\alpha=1\), then \(S=1\). But
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge w(r)-m\ge1.
\]
Thus equality holds throughout. If \(m=0\), then \(w(r)=1\). If \(m>0\), then every \(\alpha_i=1\). Applying the same argument recursively to a child branch, and using finiteness of \(C\), we eventually reach a one-vertex branch whose root has weight \(1\). Therefore \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. This proves the final assertion of Lemma 2. \(\square\)

### Lemma 3. The global decomposition at the unique bad vertex

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex of \(T\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\). Let \(C\) range over the components of \(T-b\), and let \(r_C\) be the vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\). Let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), and define
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_C \alpha_C<w(b).
\]
Moreover, if
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C
\]
with \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and \(x_C\in L(C)\), and if \(c_C\) denotes the coefficient of \(r_C\) in \(x_C\), then
\[
x\cdot(b-x)
=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr).
\]

**Proof.** Order the basis of \(L(T)\) by putting \(b\) first, followed by the vertices of the components \(C\). The full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_C A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_{r_C}\). Since the full form is positive definite, its Schur complement at \(b\) is positive:
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
=
w(b)-\sum_C\alpha_C
>0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C<w(b).
\]

Now let
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C.
\]
Since different components of \(T-b\) are orthogonal to each other and \(b\) is adjacent in \(C\) only to \(r_C\),
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_C c_C,
\]
and
\[
x^2=k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C x_C^2.
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_C c_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C x_C^2\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves Lemma 3. \(\square\)

### Completion of the proof

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\).

If \(T\) contains a weight-one vertex, then Lemma 1 gives an irreducible vertex, and we are done. So assume from now on that \(T\) has no weight-one vertices.

We claim that \(b\) itself is irreducible. Suppose not. Then there exists \(x\in L(T)\) with
\[
x\ne0,\qquad b-x\ne0,
\qquad
x\cdot(b-x)\ge0.
\]
Write
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C
\]
as in Lemma 3.

Because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every vertex \(u\ne b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]
Therefore each component \(C\) of \(T-b\), rooted at \(r_C\), satisfies the hypotheses of Lemma 2: its root \(r_C\) has lost the edge to \(b\), so
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1,
\]
and every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\) has
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u)=d_C(u).
\]
Also \(A_C\) is positive definite because it is a principal submatrix of the positive definite Gram matrix of \(T\).

Applying Lemma 2 to each branch \(C\), with the same integer \(k\), gives
\[
(2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Using Lemma 3,
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=
k(1-k)w(b)+\sum_C\Bigl((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\Bigr)\\
&\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
By Lemma 3,
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]
If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), so the last upper bound is negative, contradicting \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\). Hence \(k=0\) or \(k=1\).

For \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), the same estimate gives
\[
x\cdot(b-x)\le0.
\]
Since \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\), equality holds. In Lemma 2, the branch inequality has right-hand side \(k(k-1)\alpha_C=0\), so each branch term is \(\le0\). Their sum is \(0\), hence equality holds in every branch. Since \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, no branch \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. By the equality statement in Lemma 2, each \(x_C=0\).

Thus \(x=0\) if \(k=0\), and \(x=b\) if \(k=1\). Both possibilities contradict \(x\ne0\) and \(b-x\ne0\). Therefore \(b\) is irreducible.

Consequently \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. \(\square\)

Produce a fully self-contained proof of the following theorem. The theorem is: Let T be a finite weighted tree with vertex set V, edge set E, integer weight function w, and lattice L(T) freely generated by V with u·u = w(u), u·v = -1 if u and v are adjacent, and u·v = 0 otherwise. Assume this symmetric form is positive definite. Assume there is a unique vertex b, meaning exactly one vertex, with w(b) < d_T(b). Prove that T contains a vertex u that is irreducible, i.e. u cannot be written as u = a + c with a and c nonzero lattice elements and a·c >= 0. The proof must be understandable without any reference to prior solver outputs. Include all necessary lemmas inline: positive integral norm and weight-one irreducibility; the rooted nonbad branch lemma via the dual-parity inequality; the derived branch inequality (2k-1)c - Q <= k(k-1) alpha with equality cases; the k = 0,1 equality analysis forcing a weight-one vertex for nonzero equality; the Schur complement inequality sum alpha_C < w(b) after deleting the unique bad vertex; and the final calculation proving b is irreducible when no weight-one vertex exists. Define every notation before use, including rooted branches, root coefficient c, branch norm Q, alpha = (A^{-1})_{rr}, and the integer k coefficient of b. Do not refer to previous tasks, shared notes, or the problem above; state the theorem and proof cleanly from scratch.
