## Solver Notes (exploration log — not yet verified unless tagged)
  • [arXiv:1612.04921 Proposition 3.2] Let L=∆(p,q) be a positive-definite D-type lattice with vertex basis x_*, x_**, x_0, …, x_n. Thus L is the free abelian group on these generators with symmetric bilinear form determined by x_*·x_*=x_**·x_**=2, x_i·x_i=a_i for 0≤i≤n, x_*·x_0=x_**·x_0=−1, x_i·x_j=−1 when |i−j|=1 and 0≤i,j≤n, and all other pairings between distinct basis elements equal to 0; the integers a_i satisfy a_i≥2. An element ℓ of a positive-definite lattice is called irreducible if there do not exist nonzero lattice elements a,b with ℓ=a+b and a·b≥0. Then every vertex-basis element x_*, x_**, x_0, …, x_n is irreducible in L.  (from: literature_1612.04921)
    Proof sketch: Fix a generator x_i and take any w∈L with w≠0,x_i. Write w=w_*x_*+w_**x_**+Σ_{j=0}^n w_j x_j, and examine w·(x_i−w). Treating the diagonal entries a_j as variables, the coefficient of a_i is w_i(1−w_i), and the coefficient of a_j for j≠i is −w_j^2; since all w_j are integers, these coefficients are ≤0. Hence w·(x_i−w) is non-increasing as any a_j is increased. It therefore suffices to prove negativity in the minimal case a_0=…=a_n=2. In that case L is identified with the standard root lattice D_{n+3}={integer vectors in Z^{n+3} with even coordinate sum} by x_*↦e_{−2}+e_{−1}, x_**↦−e_{−2}+e_{−1}, and x_j↦−e_{j−1}+e_j for j≥0. Each generator maps to a root ±e_r±e_s of norm 2. If such a root decomposed as α+β with α,β nonzero in D_{n+3} and α·β≥0, then 2=|α+β|=|α|+|β|+2α·β≥2+2, since every nonzero vector of D_{n+3} has norm at least 2. This is impossible. Thus w·(x_i−w)<0 in the all-2 case, and by monotonicity also in the original lattice, proving irreducibility.
  • [arXiv:1612.04921 Proposition 3.5] In the D-type lattice L=∆(p,q) described above, for any subset A of the vertex-basis index set {*, **, 0, …, n}, write [A]=Σ_{i∈A} x_i. Let the pairing graph on A have vertex set A and an edge between two indices whenever the corresponding basis elements have nonzero pairing. An element x∈L is called an interval if x=[A] for some A such that this pairing graph on A is connected and A does not contain both * and **. Then every interval is irreducible. In w·(x−w), the coefficient of the diagonal variable a_i is w_i(c_i−w_i), which is ≤0 for every integer w_i because c_i is 0 or 1. Hence the same diagonal-weight monotonicity as in Proposition 3.2 reduces the irreducibility check to the case a_i=2 for all i. Under the standard embedding of this all-2 D-type lattice into D_{n+3}⊂Z^{n+3}, a connected interval not using both fork vertices telescopes to a single root ±e_r±e_s: along the linear chain the intermediate basis vectors cancel, and including exactly one of x_* or x_** only changes the sign/choice of the left endpoint. Such a root has norm 2 and is irreducible in D_{n+3} by the same minimal-norm argument used in Proposition 3.2. Therefore no decomposition of [A] with nonnegative pairing exists in the all-2 case, and monotonicity gives the same conclusion for the original weights.
  • [arXiv:1612.04921 Proposition 3.9] Let L=∆(p,q) be a D-type lattice with vertex basis x_*, x_**, x_0, …, x_n as above, and let an interval mean an element [A]=Σ_{i∈A}x_i where A has connected pairing graph and does not contain both * and **. Let m be the smallest index with a_m≥3. For 0≤j≤m define an involution τ_j on the vertex basis by τ_j(x_*)=x_**, τ_j(x_**)=x_*, τ_j(x_i)=x_i for 0≤i<j, τ_j(x_j)=−x_*−x_**−2x_0−⋯−2x_{j−1}−x_j, and τ_j(x_i)=−x_i for i>j. Then for every nonzero irreducible element x∈L, either x or −x is an interval, or there exists some 0≤j≤m such that τ_j(x) or −τ_j(x) is an interval.  (from: literature_1612.04921)
    Proof sketch: The maps τ_j are isometric involutions, so they preserve irreducibility. Write x=Σ c_i x_i and replace x by −x if necessary so that c_0≥0 and some coefficient is positive. First eliminate negative coefficients: if A={i:c_i<0} and y=Σ_{i∈A}c_i x_i, then y·(x−y) is a sum over edges crossing from negative to nonnegative coefficients, with terms −c_i c_k≥0. Irreducibility forces y=0, so all c_i≥0. The support B={i:c_i≠0} must have connected pairing graph, otherwise x splits as a sum of two orthogonal nonzero parts. If every c_i is 0 or 1, then x=[B]; this is an interval unless B contains both fork vertices, in which case connectedness gives B={*,**,0,…,j} and −τ_0(x)=x_0+⋯+x_j is an interval. Now suppose some c_i>1. Let z=[B]. If 0∉B, then z·(x−z)=Σ(a_i−2)(c_i−1)+endpoint terms is nonnegative, contradicting irreducibility; hence 0∈B, and connectedness gives c_i≥1 for every 0≤i≤max B. Thus c_i≥c_j>1 for all i<j. Finally test the two initial intervals w_0=x_0+⋯+x_j and w_1=x_*+x_**+w_0. Their pairings with x−w_r are respectively Σ_{0≤i≤j}(a_i−2)(c_i−1) ∓(c_*+c_**−c_0)+(c_j−2)−c_{j+1}. Since one choice of sign makes the middle term nonnegative, irreducibility forces all nonnegative terms to vanish and c_{j+1}=1. Hence a_i=2 for i≤j, c_j=2, c_0=c_*+c_**, and j<m. Applying −τ_{j+1} kills the coefficient at x_j while leaving a nonzero coefficient at x_{j+1}; connected-support and 0/1-coefficient arguments then show −τ_{j+1}(x) is an interval.
  • [arXiv:1010.6257 Proposition 3.3] Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected loopless undirected multigraph, with e(u,v) denoting the number of edges between distinct vertices u and v and d(v) the degree of v. Let Γ̄(G) be the free abelian group on symbols [v], v∈V, with symmetric bilinear form defined by ⟨[v],[v]⟩=d(v) and ⟨[u],[v]⟩=−e(u,v) for u≠v. Its radical is generated by [V]=Σ_{v∈V}[v], and the graph lattice is Γ(G)=Γ̄(G)/ℤ[V]. For T⊂V, write [T]=Σ_{v∈T}[v] in Γ(G). A nonzero element x of a lattice is irreducible if there do not exist nonzero y,z with x=y+z and ⟨y,z⟩≥0. Then the irreducible elements of Γ(G) are exactly the elements ±[T], where ∅≠T⊊V and both induced subgraphs G|T and G|(V−T) are connected. In particular, for a single vertex u, the class [u] is irreducible exactly when G−u is connected.  (from: literature_1010.6257)
    Proof sketch: Fix a root r and represent Γ(G) by the span of [v], v≠r, so the coefficient at r is 0. Let 0≠x=Σ c_v[v] be irreducible. Replacing x by −x if needed, let c=max c_v≥1 and T={v:c_v=c}; then r∉T. Compute ⟨[T],x−[T]⟩=(c−1)d(T)−Σ_{v∉T} c_v e(v,T)=Σ_{v∉T}(c−1−c_v)e(v,T)≥0. Since [T] is nonzero, irreducibility forces x−[T]=0. For any y=Σ y_v[v], the key identity is ⟨y,y−[T]⟩=Σ_C Σ_{(u,v)∈E(C)}(y_u−y_v)^2 + Σ_{(u,v)∈E(T,V−T)}(y_u−y_v)(y_u−y_v−1), where C ranges over the connected components of G|T and G|(V−T), and the cut-edge term is oriented with u∈T, v∈V−T. All terms are nonnegative for integral y-values. If G|T is disconnected, take y to be the indicator of one component of G|T; then y and [T]−y are nonzero and orthogonal, so [T] is reducible. If G|(V−T) is disconnected, take y=−[C] for a component C not containing the root; again [T]=([T]+[C])+(-[C]) is an orthogonal nontrivial decomposition. Conversely, if both sides are connected and [T]=y+z with ⟨y,z⟩≥0, then ⟨y,y−[T]⟩=−⟨y,z⟩≤0, so the nonnegative identity forces equality throughout. Connectivity then makes y constant on T and on V−T; since y_r=0 on the connected complement, y is 0 off T and is either 0 or 1 on T. Hence y=0 or y=[T], so no nontrivial reducing decomposition exists. Portable idea: when a weighted tree can be realized as such a graph lattice by adding a root and extra root-edges, irreducibility of original vertex classes becomes a connectedness-of-complement question.
  • [arXiv:1010.6257 Proposition 3.4] Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected loopless undirected multigraph with no cut-edge. Fix a root r∈V and identify Γ(G) with the subgroup of Γ̄(G) spanned by [v] for v≠r. Let T⊂V−{r}. Suppose [T]=y+z in Γ(G) and ⟨y,z⟩=−1. Then one of the following holds. (1) The induced subgraph G|T contains a cut-edge e; if deleting e splits G|T into vertex sets T1 and T2, then {y,z}={[T1],[T2]}. (2) The induced subgraph G|(V−T) contains a cut-edge e; if deleting e splits G|(V−T) into vertex sets T1 and T2 with r∈T2, then {y,z}={[T1∪T],−[T1]}.  (from: literature_1010.6257)
    Proof sketch: Write z=[T]−y. Then ⟨y,z⟩=−1 is equivalent to ⟨y,y−[T]⟩=1. Use the same edge-sum identity as in Proposition 3.3: ⟨y,y−[T]⟩ is the sum of squares (y_u−y_v)^2 over edges internal to components of G|T and G|(V−T), plus terms (y_u−y_v)(y_u−y_v−1) over cut edges between T and V−T. Each cut term is 0 or at least 2, and each square is nonnegative. Therefore the total value 1 must arise from a unique internal edge e on which the y-values differ by exactly 1; all other terms vanish. Hence e is a bridge in one of the induced subgraphs G|T or G|(V−T), and y is constant on each component after e is removed. If e lies in G|T, let T1,T2 be the two vertex sets after deleting e. Because G itself has no cut-edge, each Ti has at least one edge to V−T. Vanishing of all cut-edge terms forces the y-values on T1 and T2 to be 0 and 1 in some order, while y is 0 on the component of V−T containing the root. Thus y=[T1] or [T2], and the decomposition is [T]=[T1]+[T2]. If e lies in G|(V−T), let T2 be the component containing r and T1 the other component. Again, since e is not a global cut-edge, both T1 and T2 connect to T; the vanishing conditions force either y=−[T1] or y=[T∪T1]. This gives the second listed form. The no-cut-edge hypothesis is used exactly to force the existence of edges from both sides of the local bridge to the opposite side of the cut T,V−T, which pins down the constants.
  • [arXiv:1010.6257 Corollary 3.5] Let L=⊕_k L_k be an orthogonal direct sum of linear lattices. Here each L_k has an ordered basis x_1,…,x_n with ⟨x_i,x_i⟩=a_i≥2, ⟨x_i,x_{i+1}⟩=−1, and all other off-diagonal pairings 0. In one summand, an interval means a nonempty consecutive set T={x_i,…,x_j}, and [T]=Σ_{x∈T}x. If two intervals T,T′ in the same summand overlap but do not have the same left endpoint or the same right endpoint, write T⋔T′. A vector v is breakable if v=y+z with |y|,|z|≥3 and ⟨y,z⟩=−1; otherwise it is unbreakable. Then: (1) the irreducible vectors in L are precisely the vectors ±[T], where T is an interval in one summand L_k; (2) each summand L_k is indecomposable as an orthogonal direct sum of nonzero lattices; (3) if T⋔T′, then [T−T′]+[T′−T] and [T−T′]−[T′−T] are reducible, interpreting [A] as the sum over the basis elements in A; (4) [T] is unbreakable if and only if T contains at most one basis vertex x_i with a_i≥3, equivalently at most one vertex of degree at least 3 in the associated graph model.  (from: literature_1010.6257)
    Proof sketch: Model a linear lattice as a graph lattice: take a root r, a chain of vertices x_1,…,x_n, edges x_i x_{i+1}, and enough parallel edges from r to x_i so that the degree of x_i is a_i. An orthogonal sum of linear lattices is obtained by wedging the corresponding chains at the same root. Applying Proposition 3.3, a connected cut T not containing the root whose complement is connected must lie entirely in one chain and must be a consecutive interval; this proves the classification of irreducibles. For indecomposability, the vertex basis elements x_i are irreducible and their pairing graph is connected within each chain. In any orthogonal decomposition, an irreducible vector lies wholly in one summand; connectivity forces all x_i in a chain into the same summand, so L_k is indecomposable. For (3), if overlapping intervals T,T′ do not share an endpoint, their symmetric difference is a union of two separated pieces in the chain (or one interval split into two separated pieces when one interval lies strictly inside the other). The corresponding two nonzero sums are orthogonal, so either sign choice gives a nontrivial decomposition with inner product 0, hence a reducible vector. For (4), if T contains two vertices of degree at least 3, split T at an edge between them into consecutive intervals T1,T2, each with norm at least 3; then [T]=[T1]+[T2] and ⟨[T1],[T2]⟩=−1, so [T] is breakable. Conversely, if [T]=y+z is a breaking with ⟨y,z⟩=−1, Proposition 3.4 applies to the associated graph, which has no cut-edge. The alternative in which the bridge lies in the complement of T gives one side of norm 2, so it cannot be a breaking with both norms at least 3. Thus the bridge lies inside T, splitting it into two intervals both of norm at least 3; each such interval must contain a degree ≥3 vertex. Hence T contains at least two high-degree vertices. This proves the equivalence.
  • [arXiv:1412.0906 Lemma 3.1] Let G=(V,E) be a finite connected undirected graph with no self-loops, allowing multiple edges. Let Λ(G) be the graph lattice: start with the free abelian group on V, impose the relation [V]=Σ_{v∈V}v=0, and use the bilinear form induced by u·u=d(u) and u·v=-e(u,v) for u≠v, where d(u) is the valence of u and e(u,v) is the number of edges between u and v. For R⊆V write [R] for the class of Σ_{v∈R}v in Λ(G). A nonzero vector z in a positive-definite lattice is called irreducible if there do not exist nonzero vectors x,y with z=x+y and x·y≥0. Then a nonzero vector z∈Λ(G) is irreducible if and only if z=[R] for some nonempty proper subset R⊂V such that both induced subgraphs G[R] and G[V\R] are connected.  (from: literature_1412.0906)
    Proof sketch: Use the Laplacian/energy model of the graph lattice: an element is an integer-valued function on V modulo constants, and the pairing is Σ_{edges ab}(f(a)-f(b))(g(a)-g(b)). For necessity, represent z by an integer function f with min f=0. If max f=m≥2, let R_i={v: f(v)≥i}. Then z=Σ_{i=1}^m [R_i]. Since R_j⊆R_i for i<j, one has [R_i]·[R_j]=e(R_j,V\R_i)≥0; hence z=[R_1]+Σ_{i≥2}[R_i] is a nontrivial decomposition with nonnegative pairing, contradicting irreducibility. Thus max f=1 and z=[R]. If G[R] is disconnected, split R=A⊔B with no edges between A and B; then [R]=[A]+[B] and [A]·[B]=0. If G[V\R] is disconnected, use [R]=-[V\R] and the same argument on the complement. Hence both sides must be connected. Conversely, suppose R and V\R are connected and [R]=a+b. Choose integer functions f,g with f+g=1_R modulo constants. For each edge, orient so δ(1_R) is 0 or 1. The contribution to a·b is δf·δg. If the edge stays on one side of the cut, δg=-δf and the contribution is -(δf)^2≤0. If the edge crosses the cut, δg=1-δf and the contribution is δf(1-δf)≤0 because δf is integral. Thus a·b≤0. If a·b≥0, every edge contribution is 0. Then f is constant on G[R] and on G[V\R], and across each cut edge the constant difference is 0 or 1. Since both sides are connected, f represents either 0 or [R] modulo constants, so one of a,b is zero. This proves irreducibility.
  • [arXiv:1412.0906 Proposition 3.4] Let L be a positive-definite integral lattice of rank r. An obtuse superbase for L is a set B={v_0,...,v_r}⊂L such that v_1,...,v_r form a Z-basis of L, v_0+...+v_r=0, and v_i·v_j≤0 for every i≠j. Given such B, define a finite undirected graph G_B with vertex set B and exactly |v_i·v_j|=-v_i·v_j edges between v_i and v_j for i≠j. Then G_B is connected, and L is isomorphic, as an integral lattice, to the graph lattice Λ(G_B).  (from: literature_1412.0906)
    Proof sketch: First prove connectedness. If R is the vertex set of a nonempty connected component of G_B, let s_R=Σ_{x∈R}x∈L. For any y∈B outside R there are no edges from y to R, so y·s_R=0. For y∈R, use Σ_{x∈B}x=0 and the absence of edges from R to B\R to get y·s_R=-y·Σ_{x∉R}x=0. Thus s_R pairs trivially with every element of B. Since B spans L and L is positive definite, s_R=0. But because v_1,...,v_r are a basis and v_0=-Σ_{i=1}^r v_i, the only linear relation among the r+1 elements of B is an integral multiple of Σ_{x∈B}x=0. Hence no proper nonempty subcollection can sum to zero, so R=B and G_B is connected. Now define a homomorphism from the free abelian group on the vertices of G_B to L by sending each graph vertex to the corresponding vector in B. The total vertex sum maps to 0, so it descends to Λ(G_B). It is surjective because v_1,...,v_r are a basis, and the ranks agree because G_B is connected. The pairing is preserved: for distinct vertices it is preserved by the definition of edge multiplicity, and for a vertex v_k the graph valence is Σ_{i≠k}(-v_k·v_i)=v_k·v_k, using v_0+...+v_r=0. Hence the descended map is an isomorphism of lattices.
  • [arXiv:1412.0906 Lemma 3.5] Let L be an indecomposable positive-definite integral lattice, meaning L is not an orthogonal direct sum of two nonzero sublattices, and suppose L has an obtuse superbase B. Let v∈B and suppose v=x+y for some x,y∈L with x·y=-1. Then there are unique vertices u_1,u_2∈B\{v} such that u_1·x>0 and u_2·y>0, respectively, and the set B'=(B\{v,u_1,u_2})∪{x,y,u_1+u_2} is also an obtuse superbase for L.  (from: literature_1412.0906)
    Proof sketch: Use Proposition 3.4 to identify L with the graph lattice of the graph G_B associated to the obtuse superbase B. The paper uses Lemma 3.2, which says that indecomposability of L is equivalent to G_B being 2-connected. Apply Lemma 3.3 to the 2-connected graph G_B, the vertex v, and the decomposition v=x+y with x·y=-1. That lemma gives a cut edge e in G_B\{v}; after removing e, the graph G_B\{v} splits into two connected sides R and S, with endpoints u_1∈R and u_2∈S. Up to interchanging x and y, one has x=v+[R] and y=v+[S] in the graph lattice. The endpoints u_1,u_2 are characterized by x·u_1=1 and y·u_2=1, and no other vertex distinct from v,u_1,u_2 pairs positively with x or y; this gives the required uniqueness. The new set B' has total sum zero because x+y=v, so replacing v,u_1,u_2 by x,y,u_1+u_2 preserves the superbase relation. The cut-edge description also gives the needed unimodular change of spanning set: R∪S=B\{v}, and the replacement is the standard graph-lattice move corresponding to splitting along the cut edge and replacing its endpoints by their sum. Finally check obtuseness. Existing pairings among vertices not removed stay nonpositive. Lemma 3.3 gives w·x≤0 and w·y≤0 for every old vertex w outside {v,u_1,u_2}. Also x·y=-1. Since B was obtuse, (u_1+u_2) pairs nonpositively with all remaining old vertices. The only delicate pairings are with x and y: the unique cut edge between u_1 and u_2 contributes a negative term that cancels the unique positive endpoint pairing, giving (u_1+u_2)·x≤0 and (u_1+u_2)·y≤0. Hence all distinct elements of B' have nonpositive pairing, and B' is an obtuse superbase.
  • [arXiv:0709.0841 §2.2.3 (Positive cones and sign of the dual basis)] Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing or resolution graph, and let L=⊕_{j∈J} Z E_j be its intersection lattice, with symmetric form ( , ). Thus the intersection matrix I=((E_i,E_j)) is negative definite, has negative diagonal entries, and has non-negative off-diagonal entries given by incidences of the graph. Put L_Q=L⊗Q. For each j, let D_j∈L_Q be the dual element characterized by (D_j,E_i)=δ_{ji} for every i. Define the effective cone L_Q,e={Σ_j r_j E_j : r_j≥0 for all j} and the numerically effective cone L_Q,ne={x∈L_Q : (x,E_j)≥0 for all j}. Then L_Q,ne is exactly the positive cone generated by the D_j: L_Q,ne={Σ_j r_j D_j : r_j≥0 for all j}. Moreover, because I is negative definite and Γ is connected, every coefficient of every D_j in the basis {E_i} is strictly negative. Consequently −L_Q,ne⊂L_Q,e; in fact, every nonzero element of L_Q,ne has strictly negative E_i-coefficients.  (from: literature_0709.0841)
    Proof sketch: The equality L_Q,ne={Σ r_j D_j} is just dual-basis linear algebra: if x=Σ r_j D_j then (x,E_i)=r_i, and conversely r_i=(x,E_i). The sign assertion is the key maximum-principle/M-matrix fact. Write A=−I. Then A is symmetric positive definite, has positive diagonal entries, non-positive off-diagonal entries, and is irreducible because Γ is connected. Hence A is a nonsingular irreducible M-matrix, so A^{-1} has strictly positive entries. Equivalently, solve Ax=e_j. The solution minimizes 1/2 z^T A z−e_j^T z; replacing z by its positive part cannot increase this functional when off-diagonal entries are non-positive, so x≥0. If some coordinate x_i were 0, the equation (Ax)_i=0 for i≠j forces all adjacent coordinates also 0; connectedness propagates this to x_j=0, contradicting (Ax)_j=1. Thus x>0. Since D_j=I^{-1}e_j=−A^{-1}e_j, all coefficients of D_j are strictly negative. The inclusion −L_Q,ne⊂L_Q,e follows by taking non-negative combinations. In the original positive-definite-tree setting, this is the same statement after negating the form on a branch: inverse matrices of positive definite Stieltjes branch matrices have positive entries.
  • [arXiv:0709.0841 §2.2.4 (Canonical liftings to the effective and nef cones)] Keep the notation of §2.2.3, and let L′ be the dual lattice embedded in L_Q. Let H=L′/L. Any other effective representative has the same fractional parts and differs from it by an integral cycle with all coefficients ≥0, so the element in Q is the unique coefficientwise minimum. The algorithm stops after finitely many steps and its final value is x_t=l_{l′}, independently of the choices made. Moreover, if mathcal L is a holomorphic line bundle on X_tilde with c_1(mathcal L)=l′, and K is the canonical cycle, with χ(l)=−(l,l+K)/2 for l∈L, then h^1(mathcal L)=h^1(mathcal L⊗O_{X_tilde}(−l_{l′}))−(l′,l_{l′})−χ(l_{l′}). In particular, the computation of h^1 for an arbitrary Chern class reduces, up to the displayed explicit lattice term, to the case of a line bundle whose Chern class is nef.  (from: literature_0709.0841)
    Proof sketch: Existence: choose an effective integral Z with (Z,E_j)<0 for every j, possible by the inverse-sign result of §2.2.3. Then for m≫0, l′−mZ is nef, so at least one effective l with l′−l nef exists. If l_1,l_2 are two such effective cycles, let l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise and write x_i=l_i−l. The supports of x_1 and x_2 are disjoint. For a fixed E_j, choose i with coefficient of E_j in x_i equal to 0; then (x_i,E_j)≥0 because x_i is effective and has no E_j term. Hence (l′−l,E_j)=(l′−l_i,E_j)+(x_i,E_j)≥0. Thus the coefficientwise minimum of two admissible cycles is admissible, giving a unique minimal admissible cycle l_{l′}. Algorithm: prove inductively that every x_i constructed satisfies x_i≤l_{l′}. If x_i≤l_{l′} but x_i+E_j≤l_{l′} failed for a chosen j, then E_j has coefficient 0 in l_{l′}−x_i, so (l_{l′}−x_i,E_j)≥0. Since l′−l_{l′} is nef, this gives (l′−x_i,E_j)≥0, contradicting the rule for choosing j. Therefore the algorithm is bounded above by l_{l′}; since one coefficient increases by 1 at each step, it terminates. Its terminal x_t is admissible, hence by minimality equals l_{l′}. For the cohomological formula, along the algorithm use exact sequences 0→mathcal L⊗O(−x_{i+1})→mathcal L⊗O(−x_i)→mathcal L⊗O_{E_{j(i)}}(−x_i)→0. The chosen inequality says deg(mathcal L⊗O_{E_{j(i)}}(−x_i))=(l′−x_i,E_{j(i)})<0, so H^0 of the curve term vanishes. Therefore the change in h^1 is minus the Euler characteristic of that curve term, equal to −(l′,x_{i+1}−x_i)+χ(x_i)−χ(x_{i+1}). Summing over i telescopes to the stated formula.
  • [arXiv:math/0310083 Lemma 5.4 and Proposition 5.7] Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with vertex set J and lattice L=⊕_{j∈J} Z b_j, where (b_j,b_j)=e_j and (b_i,b_j)=1 if i and j are adjacent and 0 otherwise. Embed L'=Hom_Z(L,Z) in L_Q=L⊗Q using the form, and order L_Q coefficientwise in the basis {b_j}. Put S_Q={y∈L_Q : (y,b_j)≤0 for every j∈J}. Let K∈L' be the canonical characteristic element, K(b_j)=−e_j−2. For any characteristic orbit [k]=K+2(l'+L), the intersection (l'+L)∩S_Q has a unique minimal element, denoted l'_[k]. If k_r=K+2l'_[k], then: (a) (l'_[k],b_j)≥b_j^2+1 for every j∈J; (b) k_r(x)≥x^2 for every effective integral cycle x∈L, x≥0; and (c) χ_{k_r}(−x)≥0 for every effective x∈L, where χ_{k_r}(y)=−(k_r(y)+y^2)/2.  (from: literature_math/0310083)
    Proof sketch: Nonemptiness of (l'+L)∩S_Q comes by adding to l' a sufficiently large integral effective cycle with all pairings strictly negative; negative definiteness of the plumbing matrix gives such cycles. For uniqueness/minimality, if l'+l_1 and l'+l_2 are in S_Q, set l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise. For each basis vector b_j, one of l_1−l or l_2−l has zero b_j-coefficient; because off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative, subtracting that effective cycle cannot increase the pairing with b_j. Hence l'+l remains in S_Q. Closure under coefficientwise minimum gives a unique minimal element. For (a), if (l'_[k],b_j)≤b_j^2 for some j, then l'_[k]−b_j is still in S_Q: at b_j this is exactly the assumed inequality, at adjacent vertices the pairing drops by 1, and elsewhere it is unchanged. This contradicts minimality. For (b), first (a) implies k_r(b_j)=2(l'_[k],b_j)−b_j^2−2≥b_j^2 for every vertex. Suppose a nonzero effective x is a minimal counterexample to k_r(x)≥x^2. For every b_j in the support of x, minimality gives k_r(x−b_j)≥(x−b_j)^2, while k_r(x)<x^2. Combining these inequalities yields (l'_[k]−x,b_j)≤0 for all b_j in the support of x. For vertices outside the support, (l'_[k]−x,b_j)≤(l'_[k],b_j)≤0 because x is effective and off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative. Thus l'_[k]−x∈(l'_[k]+L)∩S_Q, contradicting minimality of l'_[k]. Finally, (c) is the identity 2χ_{k_r}(−x)=k_r(x)−x^2 together with (b).
  • [arXiv:math/0310083 Lemmas 7.3–7.4] In the same negative definite plumbing-lattice setup, fix a characteristic orbit [k]=K+2(l'+L), let l_min=l'_[k] be the unique minimal element of (l'+L)∩S_Q, and put k_r=K+2l_min. Define S_[k]={x∈L : (x+l_min,b_j)≤0 for every j∈J}. For every x∈L there is a unique minimal element y(x)∈L such that x≤y(x) and y(x)∈S_[k]. Moreover y(x) is obtained by the following generalized Laufer algorithm: set x_0=x; if x_l∉S_[k], choose any vertex j with (x_l+l_min,b_j)>0 and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when x_l∈S_[k]. The algorithm terminates, its terminal value is y(x), and at every step χ_{k_r}(x_{l+1})≤χ_{k_r}(x_l). More precisely, for a step adding b_j one has χ_{k_r}(x_l+b_j)−χ_{k_r}(x_l)=1−(x_l+l_min,b_j).  (from: literature_math/0310083)
    Proof sketch: Existence and uniqueness of y(x) are proved exactly as for the minimal anti-nef representative: the set of elements of S_[k] above x is nonempty by adding a large anti-nef effective cycle, and it is closed under coefficientwise minimum. For the algorithm, let y=y(x). The key induction is that every intermediate x_l satisfies x_l≤y. If x_l≤y and the algorithm chooses j with (x_l+l_min,b_j)>0, then the b_j-coefficient of x_l must be strictly smaller than that of y. Otherwise y−x_l is effective with zero b_j-coefficient, so (x_l+l_min,b_j)=(y+l_min,b_j)−(y−x_l,b_j)≤0, since y∈S_[k] and (y−x_l,b_j)≥0; contradiction. Hence adding b_j keeps x_{l+1}≤y. Since coefficients are bounded above by those of y and increase at each step, the process terminates. The terminal point lies in S_[k] and is ≤y, so by minimality it equals y. The monotonicity of χ is the displayed difference formula: the chosen pairing is a positive integer, hence 1−(x_l+l_min,b_j)≤0. This is the portable sign-control feature: adding a vertex with positive anti-nef defect never raises χ.
  • [arXiv:math/0310083 Lemmas 7.6–7.7 and Lemma 9.1] Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing tree with a distinguished vertex 0, and write J*=J∖{0}. Assume Γ is almost-rational with distinguished vertex 0, meaning that replacing e_0 by some e'_0≤e_0 gives a rational graph, i.e. a graph whose canonical function χ_K satisfies χ_K(z)≥1 for every nonzero effective integral cycle z. Fix a characteristic orbit [k], let l=l'_[k] be the minimal anti-nef representative, and set k=K+2l. For each integer i≥0, there is a unique minimal cycle x(i)∈L satisfying m_0(x(i))=i and (x(i)+l,b_j)≤0 for every j∈J*, where m_0 denotes the b_0-coefficient; moreover x(i)≥0. The rooted Laufer algorithm from x(i) to x(i+1) is: start with x_0=x(i), x_1=x(i)+b_0; for l≥1, if some j∈J* satisfies (x_l+l,b_j)>0, choose such a j=j(l) and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when all pairings with vertices in J* are ≤0. This terminates at x(i+1), and for l>0 one has χ_k(x_{l+1})≤χ_k(x_l), while χ_k(x(i)+b_0)−χ_k(x(i))=1−(x(i)+l,b_0); in particular the first step raises χ_k exactly when x(i)+l is anti-nef also at vertex 0. Under the almost-rational hypothesis, the following stronger conclusions hold for every i≥0: (a) if x∈L has m_0(x)=i, then χ_k(x)≥χ_k(x(i)); (b) if y>x(i) and m_0(y)=i, then for some vertex b_j in the support of y−x(i), with j∈J*, one has χ_k(y−b_j)≤χ_k(y); (c) in any rooted Laufer sequence from x(i) to x(i+1), every step after the initial b_0-step has exactly (x_l+l,b_{j(l)})=1 and hence χ_k(x_{l+1})=χ_k(x_l) for 0<l<t. Consequently χ_k(x(i)+b_0)=χ_k(x(i+1)).  (from: literature_math/0310083)
    Proof sketch: For existence of x(i), one first constructs some cycle with b_0-coefficient i and nonpositive pairings along J* by taking a large anti-nef cycle and subtracting a suitable multiple of b_0; uniqueness follows because the set of such cycles is closed under coefficientwise minimum. Effectivity uses minimality of l: if x(i)=x_1−x_2 with disjoint effective positive and negative parts, then l−x_2 remains in S_Q, forcing x_2=0. The rooted algorithm is the same bounded-above induction as Lemma 7.4, now only adding vertices in J*: x(i+1) is an upper bound, and a chosen positive pairing forces the chosen coefficient to be below that in x(i+1). The χ identities again come from χ_k(x+b_j)−χ_k(x)=1−(x+l,b_j). For the AR refinements, use that any cycle supported away from the distinguished vertex lies in a rational subgraph of the rational graph obtained by decreasing e_0; hence its canonical χ is positive when nonzero. For (a), write any x with m_0(x)=i as x(i)−y_1+y_2 with y_1,y_2≥0, disjoint support, and no b_0 in either support. Then χ_k(x)=χ_k(x(i)−y_1)+χ_K(y_2)+(y_1,y_2)−(x(i)+l,y_2)≥χ_k(x(i)−y_1), using rationality for χ_K(y_2), disjointness for (y_1,y_2)≥0, and anti-nefness of x(i)+l on J*. Lemma 7.8 then gives χ_k(x(i)−y_1)≥χ_k(x(i)). For (b), if every removal y−b_j raised χ_k, then for all b_j in supp(y−x(i)) one gets (y+l,b_j)≥b_j^2+2. Since (x(i)+l,b_j)≤0, this implies (y−x(i)+K,b_j)≥0 on the support; summing over coefficients gives χ_K(y−x(i))≤0, contradicting rationality of the relevant subgraph. For (c), set z_l=x_l−x(i). For l>0, the chosen inequality and anti-nefness of x(i)+l imply (z_l,b_{j(l)})>0. In the rational graph obtained by decreasing e_0, these same positive intersections occur, and the sequence can be completed to Laufer's computation of the fundamental cycle. Rationality forces all positive intersections in such a computation sequence to be exactly 1; hence (z_l,b_{j(l)})=1, and because (x_l+l,b_{j(l)}) is a positive integer differing from this by the nonpositive number (x(i)+l,b_{j(l)}), it equals 1. Thus χ_k is constant after the initial step. A graph with all non-distinguished vertices satisfying −e_j≥δ_j is AR by decreasing e_0 enough, which is why this lemma is directly portable to one-bad-vertex plumbing trees after sign reversal.
  • For every rooted tree satisfying the stated positive-definite rooted nonbad hypotheses, the dual parity lemma holds: if lambda_r is odd and all other lambda_u are even, then lambda^T A^{-1} lambda >= (A^{-1})_{rr}, with equality iff lambda = +/- e_r.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: Induct on the rooted tree using the Schur complement at the root. Branch parity vectors 2mu_i +/- e_i give E_i >= |a_i|, and a scalar inequality 4S|A|+(s+2A)^2 >= 1 supplies the sharp parity gap.
  • The target odd inequality (2k-1)c - Q <= k(k-1) alpha holds for all integer k, with equality iff x = k A^{-1} e_r or x = (k-1) A^{-1} e_r is integral.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: Apply the dual parity lemma to lambda = 2Ax - (2k-1)e_r and expand lambda^T A^{-1} lambda = 4Q - 4(2k-1)c + (2k-1)^2 alpha.
  • The even inequality 2n c - Q <= n^2 alpha holds for all integer n, with equality exactly when x = n A^{-1} e_r is integral.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: Complete the square: (x-nA^{-1}e_r)^T A (x-nA^{-1}e_r) = Q - 2nc + n^2 alpha >= 0.
  • For k=0 or k=1, nonzero equality in the odd inequality forces a weight-one vertex in C; hence if all weights are at least 2, nonzero equality is impossible.  (from: t1)
    Proof sketch: Nonzero equality forces A^{-1}e_r to be integral, hence alpha=1. The Schur complement inequality S >= 1 must then be equality recursively, ending at a one-vertex branch of weight 1.
  • For x = k b + sum_C x_C, the exact formula x·(b-x) = k(1-k)w(b) + sum_C ((2k-1)c_C - x_C^2) holds.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: Compute b·x and x^2 using that b is adjacent only to the branch roots r_C.
  • For the branches at the unique bad vertex, the Schur complement gives sum_C alpha_C < w(b).  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: Write the Gram matrix in block form with b as the first block and take the positive Schur complement.
  • For every rooted nonbad branch C, alpha_C <= 1, with equality iff every vertex in C has weight equal to its degree in the original tree.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: Induct on the branch using alpha_C = 1/(w(r)-sum beta_i) and beta_i <= 1.
  • The branch lemma is true: (2k-1)c_C - x_C^2 <= k(k-1)alpha_C for every rooted nonbad branch, every integer k, and every integral x_C.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: Use an induction with the convex envelope F_alpha(d), the two-line estimate F_beta(d) >= 2cd - c^2 beta + dist(c beta,Z), and the triangle inequality for distance to the nearest integer.
  • If T has a weight-one vertex, then that vertex is irreducible.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: A decomposition u=a+c with a,c nonzero and a·c>=0 would force 1=u^2>=a^2+c^2>=2.
  • Under the unique-bad interpretation, T contains an irreducible vertex; indeed, if there is no weight-one vertex then the bad vertex b is irreducible.  (from: t2)
    Proof sketch: Apply the exact formula, the branch lemma, and sum alpha_C < w(b). For k not 0 or 1 the reducibility product is negative; for k=0 or 1 equality forces all branch parts to vanish.
  • Under the exactly-one-bad hypothesis, the original theorem is proved: if T has a weight-one vertex then that vertex is irreducible; otherwise the unique bad vertex b is irreducible.  (from: advisor_r2)
    Proof sketch: Root T at b. Each component C of T-b is a rooted nonbad branch. For x=k b+sum_C x_C, one has x·(b-x)=k(1-k)w(b)+sum_C((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2). The branch inequality bounds each summand by k(k-1)alpha_C, while the Schur complement gives sum_C alpha_C<w(b). Thus for k not 0 or 1 the product is negative; for k=0 or 1 equality forces every branch vector to vanish if there is no weight-one vertex.
  • The rooted branch inequality is available in sharp form via the dual parity lemma.  (from: advisor_r2)
    Proof sketch: For a rooted nonbad positive-definite branch C with Gram matrix A and alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}, every integral x with root coefficient c and norm Q satisfies (2k-1)c-Q <= k(k-1)alpha for all integers k. Equality occurs only when x=k A^{-1}e_r or x=(k-1)A^{-1}e_r is integral; for k=0 or 1, nonzero equality forces a weight-one vertex in C.
  • For an integral positive definite weighted-tree lattice, every nonzero lattice vector has positive integral norm, and every weight-one vertex is irreducible.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: The norm formula is integral and positive by positive definiteness. A decomposition of a norm-one vertex into two nonzero summands with nonnegative pairing would force the norm to be at least 2.
  • The rooted dual-parity lemma is valid: for a positive definite rooted nonbad branch, alpha <= 1, and every dual vector that is odd at the root and even elsewhere has dual norm at least alpha, with equality only for plus or minus the root coordinate vector.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: Induct on the branch using the Schur complement at the root. The child parity inequalities imply E_i >= |a_i|, and a one-variable scalar inequality gives the required parity gap and equality case.
  • The rooted branch inequality holds: for every integer vector x with root coefficient c and norm Q, and every integer k, (2k-1)c - Q <= k(k-1) alpha, with equality exactly when x equals k times or k-1 times A^{-1}e_r, if integral.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: Apply the dual-parity lemma to lambda = 2Ax - (2k-1)e_r and expand lambda^T A^{-1} lambda.
  • For k=0 or k=1, equality in the rooted branch inequality is nonzero only if the branch contains a weight-one vertex.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: Nonzero equality forces A^{-1}e_r to be integral, so alpha is an integer between 0 and 1 and hence alpha = 1. Recursive Schur-complement equality then descends to a one-vertex branch of weight 1.
  • After deleting the unique bad vertex b, every component rooted at its neighbor satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses, and the Schur complement gives sum alpha_C < w(b).  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: The root of each component loses the edge to b, giving the required extra +1 margin, while all other vertices keep their degrees. Positive definiteness gives the strict Schur-complement inequality.
  • If T is a finite positive definite weighted tree with exactly one bad vertex, then T contains an irreducible vertex; more precisely, a weight-one vertex is irreducible if present, and otherwise the unique bad vertex is irreducible.  (from: t3)
    Proof sketch: Write any possible decomposition witness as x and b-x, decompose x over the branches, and apply the branch inequality. The cases k not equal to 0 or 1 are strictly negative by the Schur complement; the cases k=0 and k=1 reduce by equality analysis to x=0 or x=b.
  • [VERIFIED ✓] Let T be a finite weighted tree whose lattice form is positive definite, and suppose there is exactly one vertex b such that w(b)<d_T(b). Then T contains a vertex u that is irreducible in L(T).  (from: unknown)
    Verifier's restatement of what was actually proved: The proof establishes the claimed result under the stated/explicit interpretation that there is exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\): for any finite weighted tree \(T\) whose associated integral symmetric form is positive definite and has a unique such “bad” vertex, \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex of \(L(T)\). More specifically, if \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible; otherwise the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.
    Verifier's is_relaxation verdict: False
    Proof sketch: By isolating the unique “bad’’ vertex \(b\) via a Schur‐complement decomposition of the positive‐definite Gram matrix, the proof reduces any putative splitting \(b=x+(b-x)\) to a sum of branchwise quadratic inequalities of the form \((2k-1)c_C-x_C^2\le k(k-1)\alpha_C\), where the key Lemma (obtained by a parity argument on \(A_C^{-1}\)) shows equality forces triviality or a weight‐one vertex.  Since \(\sum_C\alpha_C<w(b)\), one finds \(x\cdot(b-x)<0\) unless \(k\in\{0,1\}\), and in those cases the same sharp inequality combined with the absence of weight-one vertices forces \(x=0\) or \(x=b\), proving \(b\) is irreducible.
    Full proof on disk: final_solutions.jsonl task_id=writeup_r2_a31d9c
  • In the multiple-bad example from t2, the full form is positive definite, the vertex b is reducible, and every other vertex type r, ell_i, and p_j is irreducible.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: Positive definiteness follows from Schur complements. The reducibility of b is witnessed by z=2r+ell_1+ell_2+ell_3. Irreducibility of r, ell_i, and p_j is checked using the parity-coset criterion together with exact Schur-complement inverse entries and small integer minimizations.
  • If every reducible vertex in a positive-definite weighted tree has a parity witness with coefficient plus or minus 1 at that vertex, then the tree cannot have all vertices reducible.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: A coefficient plus or minus 1 witness orients the vertex toward an adjacent component with inverse-root entry at least 1. Following orientations in a finite tree gives a directed 2-cycle, but the two inverse-root entries across any edge have product strictly less than 1 by the Schur complement.
  • Any all-reducible counterexample, if one exists, must contain a vertex whose reducing parity witnesses all have coefficient of absolute value at least 3 at that vertex.  (from: t4)
    Proof sketch: This is the contrapositive of the unit-coefficient orientation obstruction.
  • For a positive-definite weighted tree with exactly one vertex b satisfying w(b) < d_T(b), there is an irreducible vertex; if a weight-one vertex exists it is irreducible, otherwise b is irreducible.  (from: advisor_r3)
    Proof sketch: Delete b and apply the rooted branch inequality on each component; the Schur complement gives sum alpha_C < w(b), forcing x·(b-x) < 0 except in the k = 0,1 cases, where equality analysis forces triviality unless a weight-one vertex exists.
  • The known multiple-bad example does not refute the weaker at-least-one-bad reading: its chosen bad vertex is reducible, but all other vertex types are irreducible.  (from: advisor_r3)
    Proof sketch: Exact Schur-complement computations and parity-coset checks show positive definiteness, exhibit a reducibility witness for b, and rule out parity witnesses for r, the ell_i, and the p_j.
  • An all-reducible positive-definite tree cannot have unit-coefficient reducing witnesses at every vertex.  (from: advisor_r3)
    Proof sketch: A unit-coefficient witness orients a reducible vertex toward a component with inverse-root entry at least 1; following orientations gives a directed edge u↔v, contradicting the Schur complement inequality alpha_{u|v} alpha_{v|u} < 1.
  • A vertex u is reducible iff there exists y in u+2L(T), y not equal to ±u, with y^2 <= u^2.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Given u=a+b, take y=a-b. Conversely, from y define a=(u+y)/2 and b=(u-y)/2; then a·b=(u^2-y^2)/4.
  • For a reducing vector y=s u+2X, with s odd and positive, decomposed over components C of T-u, the sharp witness inequality is sum_C(s c_C-Q_C) >= w(u)(s^2-1)/4.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Expand y^2 using u·X_C=-c_C and compare with u^2=w(u).
  • Every finite positive-definite weighted tree has an alpha-small vertex u, meaning every component of T-u has inverse-root entry alpha_C<1.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Across any edge, the two inverse-root entries have product <1. If every vertex had an alpha>=1 component, orient toward one; a finite directed tree would contain a directed 2-cycle, contradicting the product inequality.
  • Any reducing witness with coefficient |s|=1 or |s|=3 forces some adjacent component to have inverse-root entry alpha>=1.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: For s=1, c-Q>=0 and Cauchy imply alpha>=1. For s=3, positive branch contributions with alpha<1 are only 1 and each costs alpha>=1/2, so the Schur complement sum alpha_C<w(u) is contradicted unless some alpha>=1.
  • If every reducible vertex admitted a reducing parity witness with coefficient |s|<=3, then an all-reducible positive-definite tree could not exist.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: Orient each reducible vertex toward an alpha>=1 component supplied by the previous result. The directed-cycle argument contradicts alpha_{u|v} alpha_{v|u}<1 across an edge.
  • Any all-reducible counterexample must contain an alpha-small vertex whose every reducing witness has |s|>=5 and contains a high-odd resonant component with F_C>K alpha_C.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: At an alpha-small vertex, summing F_C<=K alpha_C would give sum F_C<Kw(u), contradicting reducibility. The Cauchy lower bound alpha_C>=c^2/(sc-F_C) then forces the resonance interval (k-1)c<F_C<kc for s=2k-1.
  • For the first high-odd case s=5, an alpha-small reducing witness must contain a component with root coefficient c=2, norm Q=5, contribution F=5, and inverse-root entry 4/5 <= alpha < 5/6.  (from: t5)
    Proof sketch: For s=5, K=6 and the only resonant integer below K is F=5 with c=2. Cauchy gives alpha>=4/5, while the necessary excess F>K alpha gives alpha<5/6.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Naive real Schur/Legendre bound: Using only Phi_i(2c) <= c^2 alpha_i loses the alpha/4 parity slack when the Schur complement S is nonintegral.
  • Floor-only induction on even branch transforms: Bounds like Phi_i(2c) <= floor(c^2 alpha_i) are still not sharp enough in cases where c^2 alpha_i is integral but c A_i^{-1} e_{r_i} is not integral.
  • Leaf deletion: Irreducibility in a deleted subtree need not persist after reattaching a leaf, as shown by the two-vertex tree with weights 1 and 2.
  • Minimal parity-coset witness: It yields useful constraints such as connected support and local inequalities |y·v|<=w(v), but I did not extract an independent contradiction from them.
  • Multiple-bad generalization: Branches may have alpha>1 and nonzero equality vectors; an explicit multiple-bad example makes the chosen bad vertex reducible.
  • Prove the chosen bad vertex is irreducible without uniqueness of the bad vertex.: t2 gives an explicit positive-definite multiple-bad tree in which the selected bad vertex is reducible, so uniqueness is essential for that stronger claim.
  • Drop the uniqueness of the bad vertex: Then a component after deleting the chosen vertex may not satisfy the rooted nonbad hypotheses, and alpha_C can exceed 1.
  • Use only the global Schur-complement inequality for k=0 and k=1: The global upper bound is zero in these cases, so a separate branch equality analysis is needed to rule out nontrivial decompositions.
  • Use t2's multiple-bad example as a counterexample: It fails because only the selected bad vertex b is reducible; r and all leaves are irreducible.
  • Orient all reducible vertices toward efficient adjacent components: The orientation contradiction works for coefficient plus or minus 1 witnesses, but does not yet control high-odd witnesses with coefficient plus or minus 3, plus or minus 5, and so on.
  • Extend the dual parity lemma from nonbad rooted branches to arbitrary bad rooted branches: The dual parity inequality can fail for bad rooted branches with small Schur complement, even though corresponding primal integer violations are harder to realize.
  • Use the t2 multiple-bad construction as an all-reducible counterexample.: It fails because only the selected bad vertex b is reducible; the remaining vertices are irreducible.
  • Prove the weaker multi-bad version using only the unit-coefficient orientation obstruction.: The argument does not control vertices whose reducing witnesses all have coefficient ±3, ±5, and so on.

## Short answer

I did **not** obtain an explicit all-reducible counterexample. What I can prove is a fairly sharp obstruction:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree, if it exists, must contain a vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has inverse-root entry \(<1\), and every reducing parity witness for \(u\) has coefficient \(|s|\ge 5\) at \(u\).  
> Moreover, the witness must contain a genuinely **high-odd resonant branch**. For the first possible case \(s=5\), one branch must have a vector \(X\) with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry
> \[
> \frac45\le \alpha<\frac56.
> \]
> Nonbad branches from the exact-one-bad proof cannot supply such a branch.

So the multiple-bad question reduces to ruling out, or constructing, these narrow high-odd resonance branches.

---

## 1. Parity-coset criterion

Let \(u\) be a vertex. Then \(u\) is reducible iff there exists
\[
y\in u+2L(T),\qquad y\ne \pm u,\qquad y^2\le u^2.
\]

Indeed, if \(u=a+b\) with \(a,b\ne0\) and \(a\cdot b\ge0\), set \(y=a-b=2a-u\in u+2L(T)\). Then
\[
y^2=(a-b)^2=(a+b)^2-4a\cdot b=u^2-4a\cdot b\le u^2.
\]
Conversely, if such \(y\) exists, then
\[
a=\frac{u+y}{2},\qquad b=\frac{u-y}{2}
\]
are nonzero lattice vectors, \(u=a+b\), and
\[
a\cdot b=\frac{u^2-y^2}{4}\ge0.
\]

Also, any weight-one vertex is automatically irreducible, since a norm-one vector cannot split into two nonzero positive-norm vectors with nonnegative pairing. Thus an all-reducible counterexample has all weights \(\ge2\).

---

## 2. Decomposition of a witness at a vertex

Fix a vertex \(u\), put \(m=w(u)=u^2\), and write a parity witness as
\[
y=s u+2X,\qquad s\ \text{odd}.
\]
Replacing \(y\) by \(-y\) if necessary, assume \(s>0\). Let the components of \(T-u\) be \(C\). For each component, let \(r_C\) be the root adjacent to \(u\), write \(X_C\in L(C)\), and define
\[
c_C=(X_C)_{r_C},\qquad Q_C=X_C^2.
\]
Then
\[
y^2=m s^2+4\sum_C Q_C-4s\sum_C c_C.
\]
Set
\[
K=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
The condition \(y^2\le m\) is exactly
\[
\sum_C (s c_C-Q_C)\ge mK. \tag{1}
\]

For each component define the inverse-root entry
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Positive definiteness of \(T\) gives the Schur complement inequality
\[
\Gamma_u:=\sum_C\alpha_C<m. \tag{2}
\]
Also Cauchy gives
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C. \tag{3}
\]
Consequently,
\[
s c_C-Q_C\le \frac{s^2}{4}\alpha_C,
\]
and summing over \(C\) gives the necessary real bound
\[
\Gamma_u\ge m\left(1-\frac1{s^2}\right). \tag{4}
\]
Thus high-odd witnesses can occur only at vertices whose Schur complement \(m-\Gamma_u\) is very small.

---

## 3. There is always an \(\alpha\)-small vertex

For an oriented edge \(u\to v\), let \(\alpha_{v|u}\) be the inverse-root entry of the component containing \(v\) after deleting the edge \(uv\), rooted at \(v\). If deleting \(uv\) gives two rooted components with inverse-root entries \(\alpha_{u|v}\) and \(\alpha_{v|u}\), then positive definiteness gives
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\alpha_{v|u}<1. \tag{5}
\]

Therefore, in any finite positive-definite tree, there is some vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has
\[
\alpha_C<1.
\]
Indeed, if every vertex had some adjacent component with \(\alpha\ge1\), orient each vertex toward one such component. A finite directed tree with outdegree at least one has a directed \(2\)-cycle, forcing both inverse-root entries across one edge to be \(\ge1\), contradicting (5).

Call such a vertex **\(\alpha\)-small**.

---

## 4. Low-odd witnesses force an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction

Let \(u\) be reducible.

### Unit witnesses: \(s=1\)

If \(s=1\), then \(K=0\), and reducibility requires
\[
\sum_C(c_C-Q_C)\ge0.
\]
If some nonzero component has \(c_C-Q_C\ge0\), then \(Q_C\le c_C\), so \(c_C>0\), and by Cauchy,
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C\le \alpha_C c_C.
\]
Thus \(\alpha_C\ge c_C\ge1\).

So any \(s=\pm1\) witness points toward a component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Cubic witnesses: \(s=3\)

Now \(K=2\). Put
\[
F_C=3c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(\alpha_C<1\), then \(F_C\le1\). Indeed, if \(F_C\ge2=K\), then \(F_C=K\) forces \(\alpha_C\ge1\) by the integer maximum
\[
\max_{c\in\mathbb Z}(3c-c^2)=2.
\]
Moreover, if \(F_C=1\), then Cauchy gives \(\alpha_C\ge1/2\).

If all components had \(\alpha_C<1\), then every positive contribution is at most \(1\), and each such contribution costs at least \(1/2\) in \(\alpha\). Since a witness requires
\[
\sum_C F_C\ge2m,
\]
we would get
\[
\Gamma_u=\sum_C\alpha_C\ge m,
\]
contradicting \(\Gamma_u<m\).

Thus any \(s=\pm3\) witness also forces some component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Consequence

If every reducible vertex admitted a witness with coefficient \(|s|\le3\), then choosing an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction from every vertex would give the same directed-cycle contradiction as above. Hence:

> If an all-reducible counterexample exists, then at an \(\alpha\)-small vertex every reducing witness must have \(|s|\ge5\).

This strictly strengthens the unit-coefficient obstruction from Task t4.

---

## 5. Sharp high-odd obstruction

Let \(s=2k-1\ge5\), so
\[
K=k(k-1)=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
For a component \(C\), write
\[
F_C=s c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(F_C=t>0\), then \(c_C>0\), \(Q_C=sc_C-t\), and Cauchy gives
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t):=
\min_{\substack{c\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\\ sc>t}}
\frac{c^2}{sc-t}. \tag{6}
\]

Now suppose \(u\) is \(\alpha\)-small and has a reducing witness with coefficient \(s\). Since \(\Gamma_u<m\), if every component satisfied
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
then summing would give
\[
\sum_C F_C\le K\Gamma_u<Km,
\]
contradicting (1). Therefore some component must satisfy
\[
F_C>K\alpha_C. \tag{7}
\]

For such a component, \(t=F_C\) must be a **resonant integer**. Indeed, if \(\rho_s(t)\ge t/K\), then
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t)\ge \frac{t}{K},
\]
contradicting \(t=F_C>K\alpha_C\). The inequality \(\rho_s(t)<t/K\) is equivalent to the existence of an integer \(c>0\) such that
\[
(k-1)c<t<kc. \tag{8}
\]
So a high-odd counterexample must contain a branch contribution lying strictly between two consecutive multiples \((k-1)c\) and \(kc\).

### First possible case: \(s=5\)

Here \(k=3\), \(K=6\). The only resonant integer \(t<K\) is
\[
t=5,
\]
with \(c=2\). Therefore an \(\alpha\)-small \(s=5\) witness must contain a component with
\[
F_C=5,\qquad c_C=2,\qquad Q_C=5,
\]
and
\[
\frac45\le \alpha_C<\frac56. \tag{9}
\]
The lower bound is Cauchy:
\[
\alpha_C\ge\frac{c_C^2}{Q_C}=\frac45,
\]
and the upper bound is exactly \(F_C>K\alpha_C\), i.e.
\[
5>6\alpha_C.
\]

This is a very narrow target. Nonbad rooted branches from the exact-one-bad proof satisfy the stronger inequality
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
so they cannot supply such a component. Thus any all-reducible counterexample must use a genuinely bad rooted component realizing this high-odd resonance.

---

## 6. Minimality of the witness does not by itself kill high-odd cases

Let
\[
P=\sum_C c_C.
\]
If \(y=s u+2X\) is locally shortest among vectors obtained by replacing \(s\) by \(s\pm2\), then comparing norms gives
\[
\frac{m(s-1)}2\le P\le \frac{m(s+1)}2. \tag{10}
\]
For \(s=5\), this is
\[
2m\le P\le3m.
\]
The resonant atom above has \(F=5\) and \(c=2\), so its ratio \(c/F=2/5\). A formal collection with total contribution \(6m\) would have \(P=(2/5)6m=12m/5\), which lies inside \([2m,3m]\). Thus minimality alone does **not** force \(s=\pm1\) or \(\pm3\); one really has to rule out or realize the resonant branches.

---

## Current conclusion

I cannot certify an all-reducible counterexample from the available examples. The strongest rigorous reduction I get is:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree with at least one bad vertex must have an \(\alpha\)-small vertex whose reducing witnesses all have \(|s|\ge5\). For the first possible high-odd case \(s=5\), a branch must contain an integral vector with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry in the narrow interval \([4/5,5/6)\).

So the remaining problem is precise: either prove that no rooted positive-definite weighted tree can realize these resonant windows, or construct enough such branches to build and verify a global counterexample.

Let \(T=(V,E)\) be a finite tree, let \(w:V\to\mathbb Z\), and let \(d_T(v)\) denote the degree of \(v\) in \(T\). Let
\[
L(T)=\bigoplus_{v\in V}\mathbb Z v
\]
be equipped with the symmetric bilinear form determined by
\[
u\cdot u=w(u),\qquad
u\cdot v=-1\text{ if }u\ne v\text{ are adjacent},\qquad
u\cdot v=0\text{ otherwise}.
\]
Assume this form is positive definite. Assume moreover that there is exactly one vertex \(b\in V\) such that
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Call \(y\in L(T)\) irreducible if there do not exist nonzero \(a,c\in L(T)\) with
\[
y=a+c
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]
We prove that \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, then the unique vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\) is irreducible.

For \(x\in L(T)\), write \(x^2=x\cdot x\).

### 1. Positive integral norms and weight-one vertices

If
\[
x=\sum_{v\in V}n_vv,\qquad n_v\in\mathbb Z,
\]
then
\[
x^2=\sum_{v\in V}n_v^2w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E}n_un_v.
\]
Thus \(x^2\in\mathbb Z\). Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\in L(T)\) satisfies
\[
x^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

Suppose \(u\in V\) has \(w(u)=1\). If
\[
u=a+c,
\qquad a,c\ne0,
\qquad a\cdot c\ge0,
\]
then \(a^2,c^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), so
\[
1=u^2=(a+c)^2=a^2+c^2+2a\cdot c\ge1+1=2,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

It remains to prove that if there are no weight-one vertices, then the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

### 2. A Schur-complement fact

We use the following elementary fact. Let
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & c^T\\
c & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Define
\[
S=a-c^TB^{-1}c.
\]
Then
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & c^TB^{-1}\\
0 & I
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
S & 0\\
0 & B
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0\\
B^{-1}c & I
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Hence \(M\) is positive definite if and only if \(S>0\). When \(S>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
S^{-1} & -S^{-1}c^TB^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}cS^{-1} & B^{-1}+B^{-1}cS^{-1}c^TB^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

### 3. The rooted nonbad branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at \(r\). Let \(d_C(v)\) denote degree inside \(C\). Let \(A\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), written in the vertex basis, and assume \(A\) is positive definite.

Assume
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad\text{for every }u\ne r.
\]
Let
\[
G=A^{-1},
\qquad
e_r=\text{the coordinate vector of }r,
\qquad
\alpha=e_r^TGe_r=(A^{-1})_{rr}.
\]

We prove:

1. \(0<\alpha\le1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) has odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates, meaning
   \[
   \lambda_r\equiv1\pmod2,
   \qquad
   \lambda_u\equiv0\pmod2\quad(u\ne r),
   \]
   then
   \[
   \lambda^TG\lambda\ge\alpha,
   \]
   with equality if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. For every \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), if \(c=x_r\) is the root coefficient and
   \[
   Q=x^TAx,
   \]
   then for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
   Equality occurs only when
   \[
   x=kGe_r
   \qquad\text{or}\qquad
   x=(k-1)Ge_r,
   \]
   with the displayed vector integral.
4. If \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), equality with \(x\ne0\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex.

#### Proof of the dual-parity inequality

We prove parts 1 and 2 by induction on \(|C|\).

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\). Since \(w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1=1\),
\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(r)}\le1.
\]
For an odd integer \(s\),
\[
s^2\alpha\ge\alpha,
\]
with equality exactly when \(s=\pm1\). Thus the claim holds for one vertex.

Now assume \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the components of \(C-r\). Root \(C_i\) at the vertex \(r_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\). Each \(A_i\) is a principal submatrix of the positive-definite matrix \(A\), hence is positive definite. Define
\[
G_i=A_i^{-1},
\qquad
e_i=e_{r_i},
\qquad
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: its root \(r_i\) has lost the edge to \(r\), so
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and all other vertices have the same degree in \(C_i\) as in \(C\). Therefore, by induction,
\[
0<\alpha_i\le1.
\]

Order the vertices of \(C\) by putting \(r\) first and then the vertices of \(C_1,\dots,C_m\). Then
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where the \(C_i\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_i\). The Schur complement is
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
  =w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Since \(A\) is positive definite, \(S>0\), and
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac1S.
\]
Also, because \(m=d_C(r)\),
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge (m+1)-m
=1.
\]
Thus
\[
0<\alpha=\frac1S\le1.
\]

Now let \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates. Write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
a_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula from the Schur complement,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_iE_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

For each \(i\), the two vectors
\[
2\mu_i-e_i
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
2\mu_i+e_i
\]
have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates in \(C_i\). Applying the induction hypothesis to \(C_i\) gives
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge\alpha_i,
\]
and
\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge\alpha_i.
\]
Expanding these inequalities gives
\[
4E_i-4a_i+\alpha_i\ge\alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4a_i+\alpha_i\ge\alpha_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]
Hence
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to prove
\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge\frac1S
\]
for \(S\ge1\) and odd \(s\). Put \(y=2A_0\). After multiplying by \(S\), this becomes
\[
(s+y)^2+2S|y|\ge1.
\]
If \(sy\ge0\), then
\[
|s+y|\ge |s|\ge1,
\]
so the inequality holds. If \(sy<0\), set \(a=|s|\ge1\) and \(u=|y|>0\). Then the left-hand side is
\[
(a-u)^2+2Su.
\]
If \(a=1\), this equals
\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u>1.
\]
If \(a\ge3\), then either \(u\le a-1\), in which case \((a-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>a-1\), in which case \(2Su>2(a-1)\ge4\). Thus the scalar inequality always holds. Equality occurs only when
\[
y=0
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
s=\pm1,
\]
that is, only when
\[
A_0=0
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
s=\pm1.
\]

Therefore
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge\frac1S=\alpha.
\]
If equality holds, then the scalar equality forces \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). In that case
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_iE_i+\frac1S.
\]
Equality with \(\alpha=1/S\) forces every \(E_i=0\). Since each \(G_i\) is positive definite, \(\mu_i=0\) for every \(i\). Hence
\[
\lambda=\pm e_r.
\]
Conversely, \(\lambda=\pm e_r\) plainly gives equality. This proves the dual-parity inequality.

#### Derivation of the branch inequality

Let \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\). Write
\[
c=x_r,
\qquad
Q=x^TAx.
\]
Fix \(k\in\mathbb Z\), and set
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-te_r.
\]
Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Its root coordinate is odd, and every nonroot coordinate is even. Therefore the dual-parity inequality gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge\alpha.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Thus
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge\alpha,
\]
so
\[
tc-Q\le\frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality occurs precisely when \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=ke_r,
\qquad
x=kGe_r.
\]
If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=-e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,
\qquad
x=(k-1)Ge_r.
\]
Thus equality forces one of the two stated alternatives, and conversely either integral alternative gives equality.

#### The \(k=0,1\) equality analysis

Assume \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), and suppose equality holds with \(x\ne0\). Let
\[
y=Ge_r.
\]
If \(k=1\), the equality alternatives are
\[
x=y
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
x=0.
\]
If \(k=0\), they are
\[
x=0
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
x=-y.
\]
Since \(x\ne0\), in either case \(y\in\mathbb Z^C\). Therefore its root coordinate
\[
y_r=\alpha
\]
is a positive integer. But \(0<\alpha\le1\), so
\[
\alpha=1.
\]

We now show that \(\alpha=1\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex. Using the notation from the induction step,
\[
\alpha=\frac1S,
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=1\) gives \(S=1\). But
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge w(r)-m
\ge1.
\]
Hence equality holds throughout. If \(m=0\), then \(S=w(r)=1\), so \(r\) has weight one. If \(m>0\), then equality forces every child branch to have \(\alpha_i=1\). Repeating the same argument down a child branch and using finiteness, we eventually reach a one-vertex branch whose root has weight \(1\). Therefore \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex.

This completes the rooted nonbad branch lemma.

### 4. Deleting the unique bad vertex

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex of \(T\) satisfying
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Let \(C\) range over the connected components of \(T-b\). For each component \(C\), let \(r_C\) be the unique vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\), and let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\).

Because the full form on \(T\) is positive definite, each \(A_C\), being a principal submatrix, is positive definite. Define
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]

Because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\ne b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]
For the root \(r_C\),
\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]
so
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]
For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),
\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]
so
\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]
Thus every component \(C\), rooted at \(r_C\), satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses of the branch lemma.

Order the basis of \(L(T)\) by putting \(b\) first, followed by the vertices of the components \(C\). The full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_C A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-component of \(\eta\) is the coordinate vector \(e_{r_C}\). Its Schur complement at \(b\) is
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
=
w(b)-\sum_C\alpha_C.
\]
Since the full form is positive definite, this Schur complement is positive. Hence
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C<w(b).
\]

### 5. Final calculation: irreducibility of \(b\) when there is no weight-one vertex

Assume now that \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. We prove that \(b\) is irreducible.

It is enough to prove that for every \(x\in L(T)\) with
\[
x\ne0
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
x\ne b,
\]
one has
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
Indeed, if \(b=a+c\) with \(a,c\ne0\), then taking \(x=a\) gives \(c=b-x\).

Write \(x\) uniquely as
\[
x=kb+\sum_Cx_C,
\]
where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) is the coefficient of \(b\), and \(x_C\in L(C)\). For each component \(C\), let
\[
c_C=\text{the coefficient of }r_C\text{ in }x_C,
\qquad
Q_C=x_C^2.
\]
Because distinct components of \(T-b\) are orthogonal to each other, and \(b\) is adjacent in \(C\) only to \(r_C\), we have
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_Cc_C,
\]
and
\[
x^2=k^2w(b)-2k\sum_Cc_C+\sum_CQ_C.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_Cc_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_Cc_C+\sum_CQ_C\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\left((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\right).
\end{aligned}
\]

Apply the branch inequality to each rooted component \(C\), using the same integer \(k\). It gives
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
But
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]

If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), and therefore
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
So no reducibility witness can have \(k\notin\{0,1\}\).

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the upper bound above is \(0\), so
\[
x\cdot(b-x)\le0.
\]
If \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\), then equality must hold. Since each individual branch term satisfies
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le0,
\]
their sum can be \(0\) only if equality holds in every branch.

But \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, so no component \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. By the \(k=0,1\) equality analysis in the rooted branch lemma, equality in a branch forces
\[
x_C=0.
\]
Thus every \(x_C=0\). Hence
\[
x=0
\quad\text{if }k=0,
\]
and
\[
x=b
\quad\text{if }k=1.
\]
Both possibilities are excluded by \(x\ne0,b\). Therefore every \(x\ne0,b\) satisfies
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
So \(b\) cannot be written as a sum of two nonzero lattice elements with nonnegative pairing. Thus \(b\) is irreducible.

Combining this with the weight-one case, \(T\) always contains an irreducible vertex. \(\square\)

The target lemma is **true as stated**. A slightly stronger dual parity statement gives exactly the missing odd “parity slack.”

## Theorem

Let \(C\) satisfy the hypotheses, let \(A\) be its Gram matrix, let \(G=A^{-1}\), let \(e_r\) be the root coordinate vector, and set

\[
y=G e_r,\qquad \alpha=G_{rr}=y_r.
\]

Then \(0<\alpha\le 1\), and for every \(x\in \mathbb Z^C\), with \(c=x_r\) and \(Q=x^T A x\),

\[
2n c-Q\le n^2\alpha
\]

for every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), with equality iff

\[
x=nA^{-1}e_r
\]

is integral. Also, for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),

\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

In fact, equality in the odd inequality occurs iff

\[
x=kA^{-1}e_r
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=(k-1)A^{-1}e_r
\]

as an integral vector.

## Key dual parity lemma

The main point is the following stronger statement.

**Dual parity lemma.**  
If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) satisfies

\[
\lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,\qquad \lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad(u\ne r),
\]

then

\[
\lambda^T G\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]

Moreover equality holds iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).

### Proof of the dual parity lemma

We prove simultaneously by induction on \(|C|\) that \(\alpha\le 1\) and the dual parity lemma holds.

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\), \(\alpha=1/w(r)\le 1\), and for odd \(\lambda_r=s\),

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=\frac{s^2}{w(r)}\ge \frac1{w(r)}=\alpha,
\]

with equality iff \(s=\pm1\).

Now suppose \(r\) has child branches \(C_i\), with child roots \(r_i\), matrices \(A_i\), inverses \(G_i=A_i^{-1}\), and

\[
\alpha_i=(G_i)_{r_i r_i}.
\]

Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the same rooted hypotheses: the child root gains the extra \(+1\) because its degree drops by one when the edge to \(r\) is removed. By induction, \(\alpha_i\le 1\). The Schur complement gives

\[
\alpha=\frac1S,\qquad S=w(r)-\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]

If \(d\) is the number of children of \(r\), then

\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (d+1)-d=1,
\]

so \(\alpha\le 1\).

Now take \(\lambda\) with the parity pattern above. Write

\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_d),
\]

where \(s=\lambda_r\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Let \(e_i\) denote the root coordinate vector of \(C_i\), and define

\[
a_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]

A block inverse / Schur complement computation gives

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

For each branch, apply the induction hypothesis to the two parity vectors

\[
2\mu_i-e_i,\qquad 2\mu_i+e_i.
\]

Both have odd coordinate at the branch root and even coordinates elsewhere. Hence

\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge \alpha_i
\]

and

\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge \alpha_i.
\]

Expanding gives

\[
4E_i-4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,\qquad
4E_i+4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,
\]

so

\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]

Therefore

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to use the elementary scalar inequality

\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]

for \(S\ge1\) and odd \(s\). Multiplying by \(S\), this is

\[
4S|A_0|+(s+2A_0)^2\ge 1.
\]

Set \(y=2A_0\). Since \(s\) is odd, \(|s|\ge1\). If \(sy\ge0\), then \(|s+y|\ge |s|\ge1\). If \(sy<0\), write \(a=|s|\), \(u=|y|\). Then the left side is

\[
(a-u)^2+2Su.
\]

For \(a=1\), this is

\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u\ge1.
\]

For \(a\ge3\), either \(u\le a-1\), in which case \((a-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>a-1\), in which case \(2Su\ge2u>2(a-1)\ge4\). Thus the scalar inequality holds.

Equality forces \(s=\pm1\) and \(A_0=0\). Then the earlier inequalities force \(\sum_iE_i=0\), hence every \(\mu_i=0\). Therefore \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). This proves the dual parity lemma.

## Odd inequality

Let

\[
t=2k-1.
\]

For \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), define

\[
\lambda=2Ax-t e_r.
\]

Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Moreover, \(\lambda_r\) is odd and every nonroot coordinate is even. Hence the dual parity lemma gives

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]

But

\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-t e_r)^TG(2Ax-t e_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]

Thus

\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]

so

\[
tc-Q\le \frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]

Since \(t=2k-1\),

\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1),
\]

and therefore

\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality holds iff \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then

\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]

so

\[
Ax=k e_r,\qquad x=kA^{-1}e_r.
\]

If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then

\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,\qquad x=(k-1)A^{-1}e_r.
\]

That proves the sharp odd equality statement.

## Even inequality

This one is the ordinary real quadratic bound. For \(n\in\mathbb Z\),

\[
0\le (x-ny)^TA(x-ny)
=
Q-2n c+n^2\alpha.
\]

Hence

\[
2n c-Q\le n^2\alpha.
\]

Equality holds iff \(x=ny=nA^{-1}e_r\), and since \(x\) is required to be integral, equality occurs exactly when that vector is integral.

## Equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\)

For \(k=1\), the odd equality alternatives are

\[
x=A^{-1}e_r
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=0.
\]

For \(k=0\), they are

\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=-A^{-1}e_r.
\]

Thus nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(y=A^{-1}e_r\) to be integral. Since \(y_r=\alpha\) and \(0<\alpha\le1\), integrality forces \(\alpha=1\).

If \(\alpha=1\), then in the Schur complement formula

\[
1=S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (d+1)-d=1,
\]

so equality holds throughout: \(w(r)=d+1\) and every child branch has \(\alpha_i=1\). Descending recursively, one eventually reaches a one-vertex rooted branch with \(\alpha=1/w(v)=1\), hence \(w(v)=1\). Therefore nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces a norm-one vertex somewhere in \(C\). In particular, if all weights in \(C\) are at least \(2\), then for \(k=0,1\) the only equality case is \(x=0\).
